items.answer: Docker Compose defines and runs multi-container applications using a docker-compose.yml file. It defines services, networks, and volumes — a web app might define containers for the application, database, and cache that all start together with 'docker-compose up'. In development it provides a consistent local environment matching production without installing services directly on the developer's machine. For CI pipelines Compose can spin up integration test dependencies (database, message broker) before running tests, then tear them down cleanly. Docker Compose is not designed for production orchestration — that is Kubernetes/OpenShift. Docker Compose v2 is now built into Docker CLI as 'docker compose'.

items.q: How do you reduce Docker image size?
items.hint: Multi-stage builds, minimal base images (alpine), .dockerignore, combine RUN commands, remove caches.
items.answer: Key size reduction techniques: use minimal base images like Alpine Linux (5MB vs Ubuntu's 70MB) or distroless runtime images. Use multi-stage builds to separate build and runtime environments. Chain RUN commands with && and clean up package manager caches in the same layer — cleanup in a separate RUN does not reduce size because layers are immutable. Use a .dockerignore file to exclude node_modules/, .git/, test files, and documentation from the build context. Only COPY files needed at runtime. Audit image layers with 'docker history <image>' to identify which layers are largest and can be optimised.

items.q: How do Docker volumes and bind mounts differ?
items.hint: Volume: managed by Docker, stored in /var/lib/docker/volumes. Bind mount: maps specific host directory into container.
items.answer: Docker volumes are storage fully managed by Docker — created with 'docker volume create', stored in /var/lib/docker/volumes, referenced by name. They are portable, easy to back up, and can be shared between containers. Bind mounts map a specific host filesystem path directly into the container — useful for development (mount source code so changes reflect immediately) but less portable since they depend on exact host paths. Named volumes are preferred for production database storage. In Kubernetes/OpenShift both are replaced by PersistentVolumes and PersistentVolumeClaims which abstract the underlying storage, working with cloud block storage, NFS, or Ceph without changing application configuration.

items.q: What is the difference between CMD and ENTRYPOINT?
items.hint: ENTRYPOINT: always runs, defines the executable. CMD: default arguments to ENTRYPOINT, overridable at runtime.
items.answer: ENTRYPOINT defines the executable that always runs when the container starts — it cannot be overridden with 'docker run <image> <command>' (only with --entrypoint flag). CMD provides either the default command if no ENTRYPOINT is set, or default arguments passed to ENTRYPOINT. When both are used: ENTRYPOINT is the executable and CMD is its default arguments — you can override CMD at runtime. For example ENTRYPOINT ["java"] CMD ["-jar", "app.jar"] — override with 'docker run myimage -jar other.jar'. Use exec form (JSON array) over shell form to ensure proper signal handling — shell form wraps commands in /bin/sh preventing SIGTERM from reaching the main process on container stop.

items.q: How do you manage Docker images in a CI/CD pipeline?
items.hint: Build, tag with git SHA, push to registry (Artifactory/ECR). Never use latest in production. Scan for vulnerabilities.
items.answer: In a CI/CD pipeline: Docker images are built from the Dockerfile, tagged with the Git commit SHA (immutable and traceable), and pushed to a registry such as JFrog Artifactory, AWS ECR, or Quay. Never use 'latest' tag in production manifests — it is mutable and breaks reproducibility and rollback. Include a vulnerability scan step (Trivy, Clair) that blocks promotion if critical CVEs are found. Implement a promotion strategy: images go through dev, staging, and prod registries or namespaces with approval gates between each. Artifactory stores image metadata and provides a single source of truth for all artifacts across the pipeline with full traceability from Git commit to deployed container.

items.q: How do you troubleshoot a Docker container that won't start?
items.hint: docker logs, docker inspect, check exit code, verify image and entrypoint, resource exhaustion.
items.answer: Systematic troubleshooting: 'docker ps -a' shows all containers including stopped ones with exit codes (exit 1=application error, exit 137=OOM killed, exit 127=command not found). 'docker logs <container>' shows stdout/stderr including the crash reason. 'docker inspect <container>' shows full configuration — check environment variables, volume mounts, network settings, and the actual entrypoint being executed. For permission errors on volume mounts check the USER in Dockerfile matches file ownership on the host. 'docker run -it --entrypoint /bin/sh <image>' overrides the entrypoint to get a shell for manual debugging. Check system resources with 'docker stats' for resource exhaustion issues.

items.q: What is Docker networking and what types are available?
items.hint: bridge (default), host (no isolation), overlay (multi-host Swarm), none. Custom bridge for DNS between containers.
items.answer: Docker supports several network modes. Bridge is the default — Docker creates a virtual bridge and each container gets an IP on that subnet; containers on the same bridge can communicate, external traffic requires port publishing (-p). Host mode removes network isolation — the container shares the host's network stack directly, useful for performance-sensitive applications but reduces security isolation. Overlay networks span multiple Docker hosts (used in Docker Swarm). None disables all networking. In development, custom bridge networks are preferred over the default bridge because they provide automatic DNS resolution between containers by container name, which is essential for multi-container applications defined in Docker Compose files.

items.q: What is Helm and why is it used in Kubernetes/OpenShift deployments?
items.hint: Kubernetes package manager. Charts template K8s manifests, manage releases, enable rollbacks.
items.answer: Helm is the package manager for Kubernetes. A Helm chart is a collection of YAML templates with configurable values, making complex multi-resource deployments reproducible and parameterisable. Helm manages releases — each 'helm install' or 'helm upgrade' is a tracked release stored as Kubernetes Secrets. This enables 'helm rollback' to revert to any previous release version. Without Helm, deploying the same application to dev, staging, and prod requires maintaining separate YAML sets. Helm centralises environment differences in a values file per environment while sharing the same chart logic — the chart code runs everywhere and only configuration differs.

items.q: What is the structure of a Helm chart?
items.hint: Chart.yaml, values.yaml, templates/, charts/ (dependencies), _helpers.tpl.
items.answer: A Helm chart directory contains: Chart.yaml (metadata — name, version, appVersion, description), values.yaml (default configuration values referenced in templates), templates/ directory (Kubernetes manifest templates using Go templating syntax), _helpers.tpl (reusable template snippets and naming conventions), charts/ (dependency sub-charts), NOTES.txt (post-install instructions), and .helmignore. Templates use {{ .Values.xxx }} to reference values and {{ .Release.Name }} for release metadata. 'helm template' renders templates locally without installing — useful for debugging and GitOps workflows where rendered manifests are committed to Git for ArgoCD to apply.

items.q: What is the difference between helm install and helm upgrade?
items.hint: install: first-time deploy (fails if exists). upgrade: update existing. upgrade --install: idempotent, does both.
items.answer: 'helm install' deploys a new release for the first time — it fails if a release with that name already exists. 'helm upgrade' updates an existing release — it fails if the release does not exist. 'helm upgrade --install' is the idempotent command used in CI/CD pipelines because it installs on first run and upgrades on subsequent runs without checking release existence first. Additional flags used in pipelines: --atomic (rolls back automatically if upgrade fails), --timeout (time limit for deployment), --wait (waits until all pods are ready before returning success), and --set key=value (overrides specific values from the command line without editing files).

items.q: How do you manage different environments with Helm?
items.hint: Separate values files per environment: values.yaml (defaults), values-dev.yaml, values-prod.yaml. Layer with -f flags.
items.answer: Environment-specific configuration is managed through separate values files. values.yaml contains defaults shared across all environments. values-dev.yaml, values-staging.yaml, and values-prod.yaml override environment-specific settings like replica counts, resource limits, image tags, and external URLs. Multiple values files are layered using multiple -f flags: 'helm upgrade --install myapp ./chart -f values.yaml -f values-prod.yaml' — the last file takes precedence for overlapping keys. In ArgoCD each Application CRD points to the same chart with different values files per environment. This means the same chart code runs everywhere — only configuration differs, ensuring environmental parity.

items.q: How do you template a Kubernetes manifest in Helm?
items.hint: {{ .Values.xxx }}, {{ .Release.Name }}, if/range/with control flow, named templates in _helpers.tpl.
items.answer: Helm templates use Go text/template syntax. Values from values.yaml are accessed as {{ .Values.image.tag }}. Built-in objects include {{ .Release.Name }}, {{ .Release.Namespace }}, {{ .Chart.Name }}. Control flow: {{- if .Values.autoscaling.enabled }} wraps conditional sections. {{- range .Values.ingress.hosts }} iterates over lists. Named templates defined in _helpers.tpl are called with {{ include "mychart.fullname" . }}. The pipe operator: {{ .Values.name | quote }} adds quotes, {{ .Values.replicas | default 1 }} provides fallback. 'helm lint' validates templates and 'helm template' renders without installing — both essential in CI pipelines before pushing to Artifactory.

items.q: What is a Helm dependency and how do you manage it?
items.hint: dependencies section in Chart.yaml. helm dependency update fetches sub-charts to charts/.
items.answer: Helm chart dependencies (sub-charts) are declared in the dependencies section of Chart.yaml with name, version, and repository. Run 'helm dependency update' to download dependency charts into the charts/ directory and generate Chart.lock (which pins exact versions). Chart.lock should be committed to version control for reproducible builds. Condition and tags fields allow conditionally enabling or disabling dependencies based on values — useful for optional components like monitoring sidecars. In production I prefer minimal dependencies and manage databases and infrastructure separately from application deployments to keep charts simple and independently deployable.

items.q: How do you handle Helm chart secrets management?
items.hint: Never store plain secrets in values. Use helm-secrets with SOPS, ExternalSecrets Operator, or Sealed Secrets.
items.answer: Never store plaintext secrets in values files that go into Git. Options: helm-secrets plugin integrates with SOPS (Secrets OPerationS) to encrypt values files using AWS KMS, GCP KMS, or PGP keys — encrypted files are committed to Git and decrypted at deploy time. ExternalSecrets Operator fetches secrets from HashiCorp Vault, AWS Secrets Manager, or Azure Key Vault and creates Kubernetes Secrets automatically — your Helm chart references the Secret name without containing the value. Sealed Secrets (Bitnami) uses asymmetric encryption — only the cluster controller can decrypt, making SealedSecret manifests safe to commit. In my setup I used ExternalSecrets with HashiCorp Vault, keeping all secret management completely outside the Helm chart.

items.q: How do you roll back a Helm release?
items.hint: helm history to see revisions. helm rollback <release> <revision>. --atomic auto-rolls back on upgrade failure.
items.answer: 'helm history <release-name>' lists all revisions with their status (deployed, superseded, failed) and description. 'helm rollback <release-name> <revision-number>' reverts to a specific revision — for example 'helm rollback myapp 3'. Without a revision number 'helm rollback myapp' rolls back to the previous revision. Helm stores release history as Kubernetes Secrets in the release namespace — by default keeps the last 10 revisions. Rollback creates a new revision rather than reverting the revision counter, so the full history is preserved. In pipelines I configure --atomic on all upgrades which triggers automatic rollback if health checks fail within the timeout period, making failed deployments self-recovering without operator intervention.

items.q: What is the difference between Helm 2 and Helm 3?
items.hint: Helm 3 removed Tiller. Release data now stored as Secrets in the release namespace. Better RBAC security.
items.answer: Helm 2 required Tiller — a server-side component installed in the cluster with typically cluster-admin privileges, creating a major security risk (anyone who could access Tiller could modify any resource in the cluster). Helm 3 removed Tiller entirely — Helm communicates directly with the Kubernetes API using the user's kubeconfig credentials, inheriting proper RBAC permissions. Release data in Helm 2 was stored in ConfigMaps in kube-system. Helm 3 stores release data as Secrets in the release's own namespace, enabling namespace-scoped operations. Helm 3 also added CRD handling improvements and schema validation for values.yaml. All modern deployments should use Helm 3 — Helm 2 is end of life.

items.q: How do you test a Helm chart?
items.hint: helm lint, helm template, helm test hooks, chart-testing (ct) in CI pipelines.
items.answer: Helm chart testing has several layers. 'helm lint ./chart' checks for syntax errors and best practice violations. 'helm template ./chart -f values-test.yaml' renders templates locally to verify output without deploying — pipe to 'kubectl apply --dry-run=client' for additional validation. 'helm test <release-name>' runs test hook pods (Jobs annotated with helm.sh/hook: test) that execute integration tests against a deployed release. For CI pipelines the chart-testing (ct) tool from the Helm project runs lint, install, and upgrade tests against changed charts automatically. In enterprise setups I combine ct in Jenkins with a dedicated test namespace where charts are deployed and verified before being published to the Artifactory Helm repository.

items.q: How do you publish a Helm chart to a repository?
items.hint: helm package → helm push to Artifactory or OCI registry. Index managed by repository.
items.answer: Packaging: 'helm package ./mychart' creates a versioned .tgz archive. Publishing to JFrog Artifactory (most common in enterprise): use 'helm push mychart-1.2.3.tgz oci://artifactory-host/helm-repo' or the Artifactory plugin for Helm. Modern Helm 3 supports OCI registries — charts are pushed like Docker images: 'helm push mychart-1.2.3.tgz oci://registry/charts'. In my CI pipeline the Jenkins job packages the chart with the build version, runs helm lint and template validation, then pushes to Artifactory only if all validation passes. The Helm chart version in Chart.yaml is bumped as part of the pipeline to ensure every chart publish is uniquely versioned and traceable.

items.q: What are Helm values and how do you override them?
items.hint: values.yaml defaults. Override with -f file.yaml or --set key=value on CLI. Last one wins.
items.answer: Values are the configuration inputs to a Helm chart with defaults in values.yaml. Referenced in templates as {{ .Values.key }}. Override priority (lowest to highest): values.yaml defaults, additional -f files applied left to right, --set flags on the command line. --set uses dot notation: '--set image.tag=1.2.3', '--set ingress.enabled=true'. For lists: '--set ingress.hosts[0]=myapp.example.com'. --set-string forces string type for values that look numeric. --set-file loads file content as a value — useful for certificates or large config blocks. In production never use --set for secrets — use an encrypted values file with SOPS or inject from Vault at deploy time to avoid secrets appearing in shell history or pipeline logs.

items.q: What are Helm hooks and when do you use them?
items.hint: Annotations that run Jobs at lifecycle points: pre-install, post-install, pre-upgrade, pre-delete.
items.answer: Helm hooks are annotations on Kubernetes resources (typically Jobs) that tell Helm to execute them at specific lifecycle points rather than as regular managed resources. Common annotation: 'helm.sh/hook: pre-upgrade'. Use cases: pre-upgrade hooks run database schema migrations before the new application version starts — ensuring the database is ready before pods begin initialising. post-install hooks send notifications or run smoke tests after initial deployment. pre-delete hooks back up data before uninstall. The hook-delete-policy annotation controls cleanup of completed hook jobs. Weight annotations order multiple hooks of the same type. I used pre-upgrade hooks extensively for Spring Boot applications with Flyway database migrations to guarantee schema compatibility before new pods received traffic.

items.q: What is an Ansible playbook? Walk me through its structure.
items.hint: hosts, vars, tasks, handlers sections.
items.answer: An Ansible playbook is a YAML file defining automation tasks to run against a set of hosts. The structure: 'hosts' (which servers to target, referencing inventory groups), 'become' (whether to escalate to sudo), 'vars' (variables for the play), 'tasks' (ordered list of modules to execute — each has a name, module, and parameters), 'handlers' (tasks that run only when notified, used for service restarts), and 'roles' (reusable task bundles). For example a Tomcat deployment playbook has tasks to stop the service, copy the WAR file, update configuration, and a handler to start Tomcat notified by the deployment task.

items.q: What is an Ansible inventory and what are the different types?
items.hint: Static: INI/YAML file listing hosts. Dynamic: script or plugin fetching hosts from cloud/OpenShift.
items.answer: An Ansible inventory defines the hosts and groups Ansible manages. Static inventory is a simple INI or YAML file listing server hostnames and organising them into groups like [webservers], [dbservers], [prod]. Dynamic inventory uses a script or plugin that queries an external source at runtime — AWS EC2 plugin fetches running instances, Azure plugin fetches VMs, OpenShift plugin fetches pods. Dynamic inventory is essential in cloud environments where servers are ephemeral. Group variables in group_vars/ directories and host variables in host_vars/ keep configuration organised and avoid repetition across similar hosts.

items.q: What is the difference between Ansible roles and playbooks?
items.hint: Playbook: top-level orchestration. Role: reusable self-contained unit with tasks, handlers, templates, vars.
items.answer: A playbook is the top-level orchestration file defining which hosts to target and which roles or task lists to apply. A role is a reusable self-contained unit with a defined directory structure: tasks/ (main task list), handlers/ (restart handlers), templates/ (Jinja2 templates), files/ (static files to copy), vars/ (role variables), defaults/ (overridable defaults). Roles promote reusability — a 'tomcat' role can be applied across dozens of different playbooks. Ansible Galaxy provides community-contributed roles. In my work I maintained roles for Tomcat installation, JVM tuning, SSL configuration, and log rotation used across multiple application deployment playbooks without duplication.

items.q: What are Ansible modules and can you name commonly used ones?
items.hint: yum/apt (packages), copy/template (files), service (manage services), command/shell (run commands).
items.answer: Ansible modules are the units of work — each task calls one module. Commonly used: yum/apt for package management, copy for transferring files, template for deploying Jinja2 templates with variable substitution, service for starting/stopping/restarting services, file for managing permissions and directories, command/shell for running commands (shell supports pipes and redirects), user for managing OS users, lineinfile for editing specific lines in config files, uri for HTTP API calls. The key principle is to use modules rather than shell commands where possible because modules are idempotent — running them multiple times produces the same result without unintended side effects.

items.q: What is idempotency in Ansible and why does it matter?
items.hint: Running playbook multiple times produces same result. No unintended changes on re-run.
items.answer: Idempotency means running an Ansible playbook multiple times always produces the same end state regardless of starting state — it will not make changes if the system is already in the desired state. For example the 'package' module checks if a package is already installed before installing it — a second run shows 'ok' not 'changed'. Idempotency matters because it makes playbooks safe to re-run after partial failures, enables drift correction (run playbooks against all servers to restore correct configuration), and prevents unintended side effects from repeated runs. Shell and command modules are not inherently idempotent — add creates/removes conditions or when clauses to make them safe to re-run.

items.q: How do you handle sensitive data in Ansible?
items.hint: Ansible Vault encrypts vars files. vault-password-file for CI. Never store plain passwords in playbooks.
items.answer: Ansible Vault encrypts sensitive data within playbooks or variable files. Encrypt an entire vars file with 'ansible-vault encrypt vars/secrets.yml' or individual strings with 'ansible-vault encrypt_string'. Encrypted values are stored in the repository and decrypted at runtime using the vault password via --vault-password-file or the ANSIBLE_VAULT_PASSWORD_FILE environment variable. In Jenkins pipelines the vault password is stored as a Jenkins Secret and passed at runtime — never committed to the repo. For enterprise setups integrate with HashiCorp Vault using the community.hashi_vault collection to fetch secrets dynamically at runtime without storing them anywhere in the repository or inventory.

items.q: What is an Ansible Galaxy role and how do you use it?
items.hint: Community roles on galaxy.ansible.com. Install with ansible-galaxy install. Define in requirements.yml.
items.answer: Ansible Galaxy is a public repository of community and vendor-contributed roles. Install with 'ansible-galaxy install <role-name>' (e.g. ansible-galaxy install geerlingguy.java). For version-controlled dependency management define roles in requirements.yml with name and version, then run 'ansible-galaxy install -r requirements.yml' to install all at once. Commit requirements.yml to Git and let the CI pipeline install dependencies before running playbooks. I have used Galaxy roles for Java installation, Nginx configuration, and Filebeat deployment rather than writing these from scratch — saving significant time and benefiting from community-maintained and tested implementations.

items.q: How do you use Ansible with Jenkins?
items.hint: Ansible Jenkins plugin. Run playbooks as pipeline steps. Pass vars from Jenkins environment and credentials.
items.answer: The Ansible plugin for Jenkins adds an 'ansiblePlaybook()' pipeline step. Configure the Ansible installation in Jenkins global tools settings. The step accepts playbook path, inventory, extra variables (which can reference Jenkins credentials), and vault credentials. Jenkins build parameters can be passed as Ansible extra vars — for example passing the artifact version so Ansible deploys the exact artifact built earlier in the same pipeline. In my setup I chained: Maven build → Artifactory push → Ansible deployment playbook, with the artifact URL passed from the build stage to the deployment stage, giving a fully automated and traceable end-to-end pipeline.

items.q: What is the difference between 'command' and 'shell' modules in Ansible?
items.hint: command: no shell features (pipes, redirects). shell: runs through /bin/sh, supports pipes.
items.answer: The command module executes commands directly without spawning a shell — it does not support pipes, redirects, environment variables like $HOME, or shell built-ins. This makes it safer and more predictable. The shell module runs commands through /bin/sh, supporting all shell features like pipes, redirects, and variable expansion. Use command by default for simple commands and shell only when you specifically need shell features. Both modules are not idempotent unless you add a 'creates' or 'removes' parameter to check if the output file already exists. Always prefer dedicated Ansible modules (yum, copy, service) over command or shell when a module exists for the task.

items.q: How do you write Ansible conditionals and loops?
items.hint: when: for conditions. loop: or with_items: for iteration. Combine both freely.
items.answer: Conditionals use the 'when' keyword with Jinja2 expressions — for example 'when: ansible_os_family == "RedHat"' to run a task only on Red Hat family systems. Check registered variables: 'register: result' then 'when: result.rc == 0'. Loops use 'loop:' (modern) or 'with_items:' (legacy) to iterate over a list. Installing multiple packages example: 'loop: [httpd, tomcat, java-11]' combined with the yum module. Loop variables are accessed via {{ item }}. Conditionals and loops combine freely — loop through a list and apply a condition to each iteration using 'when: item.enabled'. This makes playbooks highly flexible for handling different host configurations from a single task definition.

items.q: What are Ansible facts and how do you use them?
items.hint: Auto-gathered system info: ansible_os_family, ansible_hostname, ansible_memory_mb. Use in conditionals and templates.
items.answer: Ansible facts are variables automatically gathered at the start of a play by the setup module — they contain information about the target host: operating system (ansible_os_family, ansible_distribution), network (ansible_default_ipv4), memory (ansible_memory_mb), CPU (ansible_processor_count), and more. Facts are used in conditionals, templates, and variable logic — for example a template can include different heap size values based on ansible_memory_mb.real.total. Disable fact gathering with 'gather_facts: no' to speed up playbooks when facts are not needed. Custom facts placed in /etc/ansible/facts.d/ on target hosts as .fact files are accessible as ansible_local variables.

items.q: What is Ansible Tower/AWX?
items.hint: Web UI and API for Ansible. Role-based access, job scheduling, centralised inventory, audit logs.
items.answer: Ansible Tower (commercial) and AWX (open-source) provide a web-based UI, REST API, and enterprise features on top of Ansible. Key features: role-based access control (RBAC) so teams only see and run jobs they are authorised for, job scheduling to run playbooks on a schedule, centralised inventory management with dynamic inventory sync, credential management where secrets are never exposed to users, detailed audit logs of every job run, and notification integrations. Tower is essential in enterprise environments with multiple teams sharing Ansible infrastructure — it prevents direct server access while allowing controlled automation. The REST API allows Jenkins to trigger Tower job templates programmatically as part of a CI/CD pipeline.

items.q: What are the core AWS services you have worked with?
items.hint: EC2, S3, VPC, IAM, RDS, EKS, CloudWatch, Route53, ALB, Secrets Manager.
items.answer: My core AWS experience covers: EC2 for virtual machine instances, S3 for object storage (artifacts, backups, static content), VPC for network isolation with subnets, security groups, and NACLs, IAM for identity and access management with roles and policies, RDS for managed relational databases, EKS for managed Kubernetes, CloudWatch for monitoring and logging, Route53 for DNS management, Application Load Balancer for HTTP/HTTPS traffic distribution, and Secrets Manager for credential management. I have also used Systems Manager for patch management and Parameter Store, and CodePipeline for AWS-native CI/CD alongside Jenkins-based pipelines.

items.q: What is an AWS VPC and how is it structured?
items.hint: Virtual Private Cloud. Public and private subnets, Internet Gateway, NAT Gateway, Route Tables, Security Groups.
items.answer: A VPC (Virtual Private Cloud) is an isolated virtual network in AWS. A typical production VPC spans multiple Availability Zones with public subnets (connected to the Internet Gateway, hosting load balancers and bastion hosts) and private subnets (for application servers and databases, not directly internet-accessible). A NAT Gateway in the public subnet allows instances in private subnets to initiate outbound internet connections. Route tables control traffic flow between subnets and to the Internet Gateway. Security groups act as stateful firewalls at the instance level. Best practice: never place databases in public subnets, restrict security group rules to minimum required ports and source IPs.

items.q: What is the difference between Security Groups and NACLs in AWS?
items.hint: SG: stateful, instance-level, allow rules only. NACL: stateless, subnet-level, allow and deny rules, numbered.
items.answer: Security Groups are stateful firewalls at the instance level — stateful means return traffic is automatically allowed without an explicit rule. They only support allow rules. NACLs (Network Access Control Lists) are stateless subnet-level firewalls — you must explicitly allow both inbound and outbound for bidirectional traffic, and they support both allow and deny rules processed in order by rule number. Use security groups for most access control — they are simpler and work correctly for stateful application traffic. Use NACLs as an additional layer to block known malicious IP ranges or meet compliance requirements requiring explicit subnet-level deny rules.

items.q: What is AWS IAM and what are best practices?
items.hint: Users, groups, roles, policies. Least privilege, MFA, no root usage, prefer roles over long-term keys.
items.answer: IAM manages authentication and authorisation in AWS. Users have long-term credentials. Groups organise users. Roles are assumed by services, EC2 instances, Lambda, or cross-account entities using temporary credentials. Policies are JSON documents defining allowed/denied actions on resources. Best practices: apply least privilege — grant only permissions actually needed. Never use the root account for daily operations — create an admin IAM user immediately and secure root credentials. Enable MFA on all human users. Use IAM roles for EC2 and services instead of embedding access keys in code. Rotate access keys regularly. Use AWS Organizations SCPs to set permission guardrails across accounts. Audit with IAM Access Analyzer and CloudTrail.

items.q: What is S3 and how do you secure it?
items.hint: Object storage. Block Public Access, bucket policies, versioning, encryption (SSE-S3/SSE-KMS), lifecycle policies.
items.answer: S3 (Simple Storage Service) is AWS's infinitely scalable object storage with 11 nines durability. Security layers: Block Public Access at account and bucket level — always enable for non-website buckets. Bucket policies control access at bucket and prefix level. SSE-S3 encrypts data at rest using S3-managed keys automatically. SSE-KMS uses customer-managed KMS keys with CloudTrail audit logging for every key usage. Enable versioning to protect against accidental deletions. MFA Delete for critical buckets requiring multi-factor authentication to delete versions. Use S3 Access Logs or CloudTrail data events for auditing. For CI/CD store build artifacts with lifecycle policies to expire old artifacts automatically and control storage costs.

items.q: What is AWS EKS and how does it differ from self-managed Kubernetes?
items.hint: Managed Kubernetes control plane. AWS manages etcd, API server, HA, patching. You manage worker nodes.
items.answer: EKS (Elastic Kubernetes Service) is AWS's managed Kubernetes service. AWS fully manages the control plane — API server, etcd, controller manager — with automatic HA across three AZs, patching, and backup at no charge (you pay only for worker nodes). Native AWS integrations: IAM for pod-level authentication via IRSA, ALB Ingress Controller, EBS/EFS CSI drivers, CloudWatch Container Insights. Managed node groups handle worker node lifecycle. Fargate profiles eliminate node management entirely for stateless workloads. Compared to self-managed: you give up some control plane configuration flexibility but gain significant operational simplicity, automatic control plane upgrades, and tight AWS service integrations.

items.q: What is CloudWatch and how do you use it for monitoring?
items.hint: Metrics, logs (Log Analytics), alarms, dashboards, Application Insights. Custom metrics from applications.
items.answer: CloudWatch is AWS's monitoring and observability service. Metrics are time-series data from AWS services (EC2 CPU, RDS connections, ALB request count) stored for 15 months. Custom metrics are published from applications via the CloudWatch API. Alarms trigger SNS notifications, Auto Scaling, or Lambda when metrics breach thresholds. CloudWatch Logs collects logs from EC2, Lambda, EKS, and application code. Log Insights provides a query language for log analysis. Container Insights provides node and pod-level metrics for EKS. In production I set alarms for EC2 CPU above 80%, RDS storage below 20% free, and custom application error rate metrics, with dashboards combining all key metrics for the operations team.

items.q: What is Auto Scaling in AWS?
items.hint: EC2 Auto Scaling Groups with target tracking, step scaling, or scheduled policies. ALB distributes traffic.
items.answer: AWS Auto Scaling automatically adjusts EC2 instance count in an Auto Scaling Group (ASG) based on policies. Target tracking maintains a metric at a target value — keep average CPU at 50%, adding instances when above and removing when below. Step scaling adds or removes specific instance counts based on alarm thresholds. Scheduled scaling adjusts capacity at predefined times for predictable load. ASGs integrate with ALB — new instances register automatically and terminated instances deregister. For Kubernetes, Cluster Autoscaler watches for pending pods and scales ASGs. EKS with Karpenter provides faster, more cost-efficient node provisioning by right-sizing instance types to pod requirements dynamically.

items.q: What is AWS RDS and what are its key features?
items.hint: Managed relational database. Automated backups, Multi-AZ HA, read replicas, automatic patching.
items.answer: RDS (Relational Database Service) is a managed database supporting MySQL, PostgreSQL, Oracle, SQL Server, and MariaDB. Key features: automated daily backups with point-in-time recovery (up to 35 days retention), Multi-AZ deployment with synchronous replication to a standby in another AZ and automatic failover (60-120 seconds), Read Replicas for read-scaling and cross-region disaster recovery, automated minor version patching during maintenance windows, encryption at rest with KMS and in transit with SSL, and Performance Insights for SQL query analysis. The managed service eliminates OS patching, backup management, and HA configuration complexity — you focus on database design rather than infrastructure operations.

items.q: What is AWS Lambda and when would you use it?
items.hint: Serverless functions. Event-driven, pay-per-execution, scales to zero. Good for automation, APIs, event processing.
items.answer: Lambda is AWS's serverless compute — you upload code and AWS runs it in response to events without managing servers. It scales from zero to thousands of concurrent executions automatically. Trigger sources: API Gateway (HTTP), S3 events, SQS/SNS messages, CloudWatch Events (scheduled), DynamoDB streams. Billing is per 100ms of execution and number of requests. Use cases: event-driven processing (resize uploaded images, process S3 file uploads), scheduled automation (nightly cleanup jobs, certificate renewal checks), lightweight API backends, and CI/CD automation (trigger deployments, send notifications). Not suitable for long-running processes (15 minute maximum), stateful applications, or workloads needing consistently low latency at very high scale.

items.q: What is the difference between horizontal and vertical scaling in AWS?
items.hint: Vertical: bigger instance (scale up). Horizontal: more instances (scale out). Horizontal preferred for availability.
items.answer: Vertical scaling increases resources on an existing instance — moving from t3.medium to t3.xlarge adds more CPU and RAM. It requires downtime for EC2 and has upper limits. Horizontal scaling adds more instances of the same size behind a load balancer. It provides fault tolerance, scales to virtually unlimited capacity, and is supported by Auto Scaling Groups. AWS encourages horizontal scaling with stateless applications — design applications to not store session state locally (use ElastiCache or DynamoDB for sessions) so any instance can handle any request. Vertical scaling is used for stateful workloads like databases where horizontal scaling requires application-level changes like sharding.

items.q: How do you implement high availability in AWS?
items.hint: Multi-AZ deployments, ALB across AZs, Auto Scaling, RDS Multi-AZ, S3 durability, stateless application design.
items.answer: High availability in AWS is built on multiple Availability Zones. Key patterns: deploy EC2 instances in an Auto Scaling Group spanning at least two AZs behind an ALB that routes to all AZs. Use RDS Multi-AZ with automatic failover. Store shared state in ElastiCache (Redis Multi-AZ) rather than instance memory to keep applications stateless. Use S3 for static assets — inherently durable across multiple AZs. Route53 health checks with DNS failover route traffic to healthy regions for cross-region disaster recovery. Use SQS for decoupling — if a downstream service is temporarily down, messages queue rather than being lost. Target 99.99% availability requires multi-region active-active architecture which is significantly more complex and expensive.

items.q: What is AWS IAM role and how do EC2 instances use it?
items.hint: Instance Profile attaches a role. Application retrieves temporary credentials from instance metadata service (IMDS).
items.answer: An IAM role is an identity with permissions but no long-term credentials — it issues temporary STS credentials valid for up to 12 hours. EC2 instances use Instance Profiles to attach a role — the instance metadata service (IMDS) at 169.254.169.254 provides temporary credentials to applications on the instance. AWS SDKs automatically retrieve and refresh these credentials from IMDS — no hardcoded credentials needed in application code or config files. For EKS, IRSA (IAM Roles for Service Accounts) maps Kubernetes service accounts to IAM roles using OIDC federation — each pod gets its own role with minimum required permissions rather than all pods on a node sharing a single node role.

items.q: What is AWS CloudFormation and how does it compare to Terraform?
items.hint: CloudFormation: AWS-native IaC (no state file). Terraform: multi-cloud, state file, rich module ecosystem.
items.answer: CloudFormation is AWS's native IaC service using JSON or YAML templates to provision AWS resources, managing a Stack with automatic dependency resolution and rollback on failure. No state file management — AWS handles it internally. Tight AWS integration means new services are supported day one. Terraform is HashiCorp's multi-cloud IaC tool supporting AWS, Azure, GCP, and hundreds of providers. It maintains a state file and uses HCL syntax. Terraform advantages: multi-cloud portability, large community module ecosystem, better plan output showing exact changes before applying, and workspace-based environment management. In practice I use Terraform for cross-cloud infrastructure and CloudFormation for AWS-specific service deployments where native integration with Service Catalog or Config Rules is needed.

items.q: What are the key Linux commands you use daily as a DevOps engineer?
items.hint: ps, top, netstat/ss, df, du, grep, awk, sed, tail, journalctl, systemctl.
items.answer: Daily Linux commands: Process management — 'ps aux | grep java' to find Java processes, 'top' or 'htop' for live CPU and memory, 'kill -9 <pid>' to terminate. File system — 'df -h' for disk space, 'du -sh *' for directory sizes, 'find / -name *.log -mtime +7' to locate old log files. Log analysis — 'tail -f /var/log/app.log' for live logs, 'grep -i error catalina.out' to filter errors, 'grep -c ERROR app.log' to count occurrences. Text processing — 'awk '{print $2}'' to extract fields, 'sed -i 's/old/new/g' file' for in-place replacement. Services — 'systemctl status tomcat', 'systemctl restart jenkins', 'journalctl -u nginx --since today'. Network — 'ss -tlnp' for listening ports, 'curl -I https://api.example.com' for HTTP testing.

items.q: How do you find and kill a process consuming high CPU in Linux?
items.hint: top to find PID, kill -15 for graceful, kill -9 for force. Take thread dump before killing Java processes.
items.answer: Finding high CPU processes: 'top' sorts by CPU by default — press 'c' to show full command. 'ps aux --sort=-%cpu | head -20' lists top consumers. Once the PID is identified, attempt graceful termination first: 'kill -15 <PID>' (SIGTERM) gives the process a chance to clean up. If it does not stop, force kill: 'kill -9 <PID>' (SIGKILL) terminates immediately without cleanup — use as a last resort. For Java processes specifically, before killing, take a thread dump with 'kill -3 <PID>' which prints stack traces to stdout — this helps diagnose what the JVM was doing when consuming high CPU, such as an infinite loop or lock contention, enabling root cause analysis.

items.q: How do you check disk usage and free up space in Linux?
items.hint: df -h for volumes, du -sh * for directories. Clear old logs, core dumps, Docker images.
items.answer: Check disk usage: 'df -h' shows filesystem mount points with used and available space. 'du -sh /var/log/*' identifies large directories. 'du -ah /var/log | sort -rh | head -20' finds the largest files. Free up space: rotate and compress logs with 'logrotate -f /etc/logrotate.conf'. Delete old log files: 'find /var/log -name *.log.* -mtime +30 -delete'. Clear package manager caches: 'yum clean all' on RHEL. Remove old Docker images: 'docker system prune -a'. Clear old JVM core dumps: 'find / -name core.* -delete'. In OpenShift monitor node disk pressure with 'oc describe node | grep Pressure' and configure logrotate and emptyDir size limits to prevent node disk exhaustion.

items.q: How do you check open network ports on a Linux server?
items.hint: ss -tlnp (modern) or netstat -tlnp (legacy). lsof -i :8080 for specific port. curl to test HTTP.
items.answer: 'ss -tlnp' is the modern command — shows TCP listening ports with numeric addresses and process PIDs. 'netstat -tlnp' works on older systems. For a specific port: 'ss -tlnp | grep :8080' or 'lsof -i :8080'. To check if a port is reachable from another server: 'nc -zv targetserver 8080' (netcat) or 'telnet targetserver 8080'. 'curl http://targetserver:8080/healthcheck' tests HTTP connectivity and response. For firewall rules on RHEL: 'firewall-cmd --list-all'. Check iptables directly: 'iptables -L -n -v'. These commands are essential for troubleshooting connectivity between application servers, load balancers, and databases in multi-tier enterprise architectures.

items.q: What is a Linux file permission and how do you change it?
items.hint: rwx for owner/group/other. chmod numeric (755) or symbolic (u+x). chown for ownership.
items.answer: Linux file permissions have three levels: owner (u), group (g), and others (o), each with read (r=4), write (w=2), and execute (x=1). Numeric mode: chmod 755 file (owner=rwx, group=r-x, others=r-x). Symbolic mode: chmod u+x script.sh (add execute for owner), chmod go-w file (remove write from group and others). 'chown user:group file' changes ownership. 'chown -R tomcat:tomcat /opt/tomcat' recursively changes ownership. In DevOps, correct permissions on deployment directories are a security baseline requirement — app server should own its webapps directory, configuration files containing credentials should not be world-readable, and scripts should be executable only by the appropriate service account.

items.q: How do you manage system services in Linux (systemd)?
items.hint: systemctl start/stop/restart/status/enable/disable. journalctl -u service for logs.
items.answer: Systemd is the standard init system on modern Linux. Key commands: 'systemctl start|stop|restart|reload <service>' manages service state. 'systemctl status <service>' shows current state, recent logs, and PID. 'systemctl enable <service>' creates a symlink so the service starts automatically at boot. 'systemctl disable <service>' removes the autostart symlink. 'journalctl -u <service> -n 100' shows last 100 log lines. 'journalctl -u <service> --since today' shows today's logs. Service unit files in /etc/systemd/system/ define ExecStart, Restart policy, and dependencies. After modifying a unit file: 'systemctl daemon-reload' then restart the service to apply changes.

items.q: What is the Linux load average and how do you interpret it?
items.hint: 1/5/15 minute averages. Compare to CPU core count. Above core count means overloaded.
items.answer: Load average shows three numbers (e.g. 2.15, 1.87, 1.65) representing runnable processes in the last 1, 5, and 15 minutes. The critical context is your CPU core count — a load of 4.0 on a 4-core system means full utilisation with processes waiting. Load above the core count means the system is overloaded. Increasing load trend (1-min higher than 15-min) indicates growing pressure. High load with normal CPU% can indicate I/O wait — processes blocked on disk or network. Check 'iostat -x 1' for disk I/O wait and 'vmstat 1' for CPU steal and wait time to differentiate CPU-bound from I/O-bound load.

items.q: How do you monitor system performance in real time on Linux?
items.hint: top/htop (CPU/memory), iotop (disk I/O), iftop (network), vmstat, iostat, sar for historical data.
items.answer: Real-time performance monitoring: 'top' or 'htop' shows CPU per core, memory, and top processes. 'vmstat 2 10' shows CPU user/sys/wait, memory, and swap — watch for high 'wa' (I/O wait). 'iostat -xz 2' shows per-disk utilisation and await time — high await above 50ms or %util above 80% indicates disk bottleneck. 'iotop' shows per-process disk I/O. 'iftop' or 'nethogs' shows network bandwidth per connection or process. 'sar -u 1 10' from the sysstat package collects CPU, memory, network, and disk stats — sar can also query historical data from /var/log/sa/ files. For containerised environments 'docker stats' or 'oc adm top nodes' give container-level resource usage.

items.q: What is a cron job and how do you set one up?
items.hint: Scheduled execution. crontab -e to edit. Format: minute hour day month weekday command.
items.answer: Cron is a time-based job scheduler. Each user edits their crontab with 'crontab -e'. Format: 'minute hour day month weekday command'. Examples: '0 2 * * * /opt/backup.sh' runs at 2 AM daily. '*/15 * * * * /opt/healthcheck.sh' runs every 15 minutes. '0 6 * * 1 /opt/weekly-report.sh' runs at 6 AM every Monday. Best practices: use full paths for commands since cron has minimal PATH. Redirect output to a log file ('command >> /var/log/job.log 2>&1') to avoid cron sending emails for every execution. Test commands manually before adding to cron. Use cron for log rotation, health checks, backup scripts, and certificate renewal automation in DevOps environments.

items.q: How do you troubleshoot SSH connection issues?
items.hint: ssh -v for verbose output. Check sshd_config, firewall port 22, authorized_keys permissions.
items.answer: SSH troubleshooting steps: 'ssh -v user@server' provides verbose debug output showing where the connection fails. Check the server is listening: 'ss -tlnp | grep 22' or 'systemctl status sshd'. Verify firewall allows port 22: 'firewall-cmd --list-all'. For key-based auth failures, check permissions on the client: ~/.ssh must be 700, ~/.ssh/id_rsa must be 600. On the server, ~/.ssh must be 700 and ~/.ssh/authorized_keys must be 600, owned by the correct user. Check /var/log/secure (RHEL) or /var/log/auth.log (Ubuntu) on the server for detailed auth failure messages. Common sshd_config issues: PermitRootLogin no, PasswordAuthentication no, MaxAuthTries 3. After config changes: 'systemctl reload sshd'.

items.q: What is SELinux and how do you troubleshoot it?
items.hint: Enforcing/Permissive/Disabled. ausearch for denials. audit2allow to generate policy. restorecon for file contexts.
items.answer: SELinux is a mandatory access control system enforcing fine-grained security policies — processes and files have security contexts and access is denied if policies do not allow it. Modes: Enforcing (denials blocked and logged), Permissive (denials logged but not blocked — for debugging), Disabled. Check mode: 'getenforce'. When applications fail mysteriously check for SELinux denials: 'ausearch -m avc -ts recent' or 'tail /var/log/audit/audit.log | grep denied'. Temporarily test by setting Permissive: 'setenforce 0'. For permanent fix: 'audit2why < /var/log/audit/audit.log' explains denials. 'audit2allow -a -M mypolicy' generates a policy module. 'restorecon -Rv /path' restores correct SELinux context on files after moving them.

items.q: How do you manage software packages in Linux?
items.hint: RHEL: yum/dnf. Debian/Ubuntu: apt. Install, update, remove, search. Internal repo mirrors in enterprise.
items.answer: Package management depends on the distribution. RHEL/CentOS: 'yum install httpd', 'yum update', 'yum remove httpd', 'yum list installed', 'yum search tomcat'. Debian/Ubuntu: 'apt-get update' (update package list), 'apt-get install nginx', 'apt-get upgrade', 'apt-get remove nginx'. Repository configuration: RHEL stores repo files in /etc/yum.repos.d/ as .repo files. For enterprise environments repositories point to internal mirrors (Nexus or Artifactory as a yum/apt proxy) rather than public internet — this ensures packages are vetted, available offline, and version-controlled. This is a standard requirement in banking and regulated industries where direct internet access from production servers is blocked for security compliance.

items.q: How do you use grep, awk, and sed for log analysis?
items.hint: grep: filter lines. awk: extract/transform fields. sed: stream edit. Combine with pipes for powerful analysis.
items.answer: Practical log analysis examples: grep — 'grep -i ERROR app.log' (case-insensitive search), 'grep -v DEBUG app.log' (exclude debug lines), 'grep -c 500 access.log' (count 500 errors), 'grep -A 3 NullPointerException app.log' (3 lines after match). awk — 'awk '{print $1, $7}' access.log' (print IP and URL), 'awk -F: '$2 > 1000' metrics.log' (filter by field value), 'awk '{sum += $5} END {print sum/NR}' response_times.log' (calculate average response time). sed — 'sed -n 100,200p app.log' (print specific line range), 'sed s/password=[^ ]*/password=REDACTED/g app.log' (mask passwords in logs). Combine: 'grep ERROR catalina.out | awk '{print $3}' | sort | uniq -c | sort -rn | head -10' finds the most common error sources.

items.q: How do you configure firewall rules in Linux?
items.hint: RHEL: firewalld (firewall-cmd). Ubuntu: ufw. Underlying: iptables/nftables.
items.answer: On RHEL/CentOS 7+, firewalld is the default: 'firewall-cmd --permanent --add-port=8080/tcp' opens port 8080. 'firewall-cmd --permanent --add-service=https' adds a predefined service. 'firewall-cmd --reload' applies permanent rules. 'firewall-cmd --list-all' shows active rules. On Ubuntu, ufw provides a simpler interface: 'ufw allow 22', 'ufw allow from 10.0.0.0/8 to any port 8080'. Both manage iptables/nftables rules underneath. For complex stateful rules edit iptables directly: 'iptables -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 8080 -s 10.0.0.0/8 -j ACCEPT'. Save rules: 'iptables-save > /etc/iptables/rules.v4'. In containerised environments Kubernetes NetworkPolicies manage pod-level firewall rules — OS-level firewall rules apply to node external interfaces only.

items.q: How do you manage Linux users and groups?
items.hint: useradd, usermod, userdel, passwd. groupadd. /etc/passwd, /etc/shadow, /etc/group.
items.answer: User management: 'useradd -m -s /bin/bash -g developers -G sudo username' creates a user with home directory, bash shell, and group memberships. 'passwd username' sets the password. 'usermod -aG wheel username' adds user to wheel group for sudo access — -a is essential to append rather than replace groups. 'userdel -r username' removes user and home directory. Service accounts for applications (tomcat, jenkins) should have no login shell (/sbin/nologin), no privileged group memberships, and be locked: 'usermod -L tomcat'. In enterprise environments users are managed centrally via LDAP and Active Directory rather than local files — local service accounts are the exception for application runtime processes.

items.q: What is a shell script and how do you write a basic one?
items.hint: #!/bin/bash shebang. Variables, if/else, for/while loops, functions, exit codes.
items.answer: A shell script is an executable text file starting with '#!/bin/bash' containing shell commands. Key elements: variables (NAME="value", accessed as $NAME), conditionals (if [ "$1" == "start" ]; then systemctl start tomcat; fi), for loops (for server in web01 web02 web03; do ssh $server 'systemctl restart app'; done), functions for reusable blocks, and exit codes (0=success, non-zero=failure). Use 'set -euo pipefail' at the top to exit on first error, catch undefined variables, and propagate pipe failures. Always quote variables to handle spaces. Use 'shellcheck' to lint scripts for common errors before running. In DevOps, scripts automate deployments, health checks, backups, and maintenance tasks across server fleets.

items.q: What is the difference between single quotes and double quotes in bash?
items.hint: Single: literal, no expansion. Double: allows variable and command substitution.
items.answer: Single quotes preserve every character literally — no variable expansion, no command substitution, no interpretation of special characters. 'echo '$HOME'' prints literally $HOME. Double quotes allow variable expansion ($VAR), command substitution ($(command)), and escape sequences but prevent word splitting and glob expansion. 'echo "$HOME"' prints the actual home directory path. Practical impact: always double-quote variable references ('"$var"') to prevent word splitting if the variable contains spaces. Use single quotes for passwords, regex patterns, or any string where you need to prevent bash from interpreting special characters.

items.q: How do you handle errors in shell scripts?
items.hint: set -euo pipefail, trap for cleanup, check exit codes with $?, || for inline error handling.
items.answer: Robust error handling: 'set -e' causes the script to exit immediately when any command returns non-zero. 'set -u' treats unset variables as errors — catches typos in variable names. 'set -o pipefail' makes pipelines fail if any command in the pipe fails. 'set -euo pipefail' at the script start is a solid baseline for production scripts. The trap command runs cleanup code on exit: 'trap 'echo "Script failed at line $LINENO"' ERR'. Check specific exit codes inline: 'some_command || { echo "Failed"; exit 1; }'. '$?' holds the last exit code. Avoid 'set -e' in interactive scripts where commands intentionally return non-zero — add '|| true' for commands where failure is expected and acceptable.

items.q: What is the use of $?, $$, $!, $# and $@ in bash?
items.hint: $?: last exit code. $$: current PID. $!: last background job PID. $#: argument count. $@: all arguments.
items.answer: Special variables: '$?' holds the exit code of the last command (0=success, non-zero=failure) — check immediately after a command as subsequent commands overwrite it. '$$' is the PID of the current shell — used to create unique temp files: 'TMPFILE=/tmp/script_$$.tmp'. '$!' is the PID of the last background process started with & — used to wait for it or kill it. '$#' is the count of positional arguments. '$@' is all positional arguments as separate quoted words — use in for loops: 'for arg in "$@"'. '$0' is the script name. '$1' through '$9' are individual arguments. 'shift' moves $2 to $1 — useful for processing variable argument lists.

items.q: How do you write a function in bash?
items.hint: name() { commands; } Local variables with 'local'. Return exit codes with return N.
items.answer: Bash functions: 'deploy() { echo "Deploying $1"; }' — call with 'deploy myapp'. Functions receive arguments as $1, $2, etc. Use 'local' to declare variables that do not leak to outer scope: 'local result=$(some_command)'. Functions return exit codes (0-255) with 'return N' — they cannot return strings. To return a string value, echo it and capture with command substitution: 'result=$(myfunc)'. A well-structured deployment script might have functions: validate_args(), pull_artifact(), stop_service(), deploy(), verify_health(), rollback() — each with clear single responsibility and proper exit code propagation.

items.q: How do you process command line arguments in a shell script?
items.hint: $1 $2 for positional. getopts for flags (-e env -v). Always validate arguments before proceeding.
items.answer: Positional arguments ($1, $2, etc.) for ordered arguments. getopts processes Unix-style flags: 'while getopts e:v opt; do case $opt in e) ENV=$OPTARG;; v) VERBOSE=true;; ?) echo "Usage: $0 -e env [-v]"; exit 1;; esac; done'. Supports './deploy.sh -e prod -v'. Always validate arguments — check $# for expected count, validate values with regex: 'if [[ ! "$ENV" =~ ^(dev|staging|prod)$ ]]; then echo "Invalid env"; exit 1; fi'. Provide a usage function called when arguments are wrong. Document expected arguments in a header comment. For complex CLIs consider Python with argparse rather than bash getopts.

items.q: What is the difference between [ ] and [[ ]] in bash?
items.hint: [ ] is POSIX /bin/test. [[ ]] is bash built-in, supports regex =~, safer with variables.
items.answer: '[ ]' (or test) is the POSIX-compliant test command — works in all POSIX shells but requires quoting variables to prevent word splitting and does not support regex or logical && operators. '[[ ]]' is a bash built-in keyword: variables do not undergo word splitting inside it (less critical to quote though still good practice), supports regex matching ('if [[ "$string" =~ ^[0-9]+$ ]]'), supports && and || logical operators, and handles empty variables correctly without errors. For bash scripts always use [[ ]] consistently — it is safer and more readable. Use [ ] only when writing POSIX-portable scripts that must run on minimal systems where /bin/sh may not be bash.

items.q: How do you create and use arrays in bash?
items.hint: arr=(a b c). ${arr[0]}, ${arr[@]}, ${#arr[@]}. Associative arrays with declare -A.
items.answer: Indexed arrays: 'servers=(web01 web02 web03)' or 'declare -a servers'. Access elements: '${servers[0]}' (first), '${servers[@]}' (all), '${#servers[@]}' (count). Iterate: 'for server in "${servers[@]}"; do echo $server; done'. Append: 'servers+=(web04)'. Load file into array: 'mapfile -t lines < file.txt'. Associative arrays (bash 4+): 'declare -A config; config[host]=db01; config[port]=5432'. Access: '${config[host]}', keys: '${!config[@]}'. Arrays are essential for storing lists of servers, environments, or filenames in deployment scripts to iterate systematically rather than hardcoding repeated logic for each server individually.

items.q: How do you redirect output in bash?
items.hint: > overwrite, >> append, 2> stderr, 2>&1 merge stderr to stdout, | pipe, /dev/null discard.
items.answer: I/O redirection: '>' redirects stdout to file (overwrites), '>>' appends to file, '2>' redirects stderr only, '2>&1' redirects stderr to the same destination as stdout (order matters — always put 2>&1 after stdout redirect). 'command > output.log 2>&1' captures both stdout and stderr. 'command &> output.log' is bash shorthand for the same. 'command > /dev/null 2>&1' discards all output. '|' pipes stdout of one command to stdin of the next. 'tee output.log' splits output — writes to file AND passes to next command. 'exec 1>>/var/log/script.log 2>&1' redirects all subsequent output in a script to a log file.

items.q: What is the use of xargs in Linux?
items.hint: Converts stdin lines to command arguments. -I{} for placeholder, -P for parallel execution.
items.answer: xargs reads items from stdin and executes a command with those items as arguments. 'find /app -name *.log -mtime +7 | xargs rm' deletes old log files. 'cat servers.txt | xargs -I{} ssh {} systemctl status tomcat' runs a command on each server. '-I{}' is a placeholder for the input item. '-n 1' passes one argument per invocation. '-P 4' runs 4 processes in parallel — 'cat urls.txt | xargs -P 4 -I{} curl -O {}' downloads 4 files simultaneously. '-d \n' sets newline as delimiter. Combine with 'find -print0' and 'xargs -0' to safely handle filenames containing spaces: 'find /logs -name *.gz -print0 | xargs -0 rm'.

items.q: How do you schedule and manage background processes in bash?
items.hint: & for background. nohup for persistent after logout. jobs/fg/bg for control. systemd for production services.
items.answer: Run in background: 'command &' — PID is printed and shell returns immediately. 'jobs' lists background jobs, 'fg %1' brings job 1 to foreground, 'bg %1' resumes a stopped job. 'wait' waits for all background jobs; 'wait $PID' for a specific PID. For persistent processes that survive logout: 'nohup command > output.log 2>&1 &'. 'disown %1' removes a job from the job table so closing the terminal does not terminate it. For production background services always use systemd service units rather than nohup — they handle restart on failure, logging via journald, and start at boot automatically without manual intervention after server restarts.

items.q: What is a heredoc in bash and when do you use it?
items.hint: << EOF ... EOF for multi-line input. Used for config file generation, SQL scripts, multi-line commands.
items.answer:
A heredoc passes multiple lines of text to a command as stdin without creating a temp file. Syntax: 'command <<EOF
line1
line2
EOF'. Variables are expanded inside heredoc by default — use '<<'EOF'' (quoted delimiter) to prevent expansion and treat content literally. Practical uses: generating config files dynamically ('cat > /etc/app/config.properties <<EOF
db.host=${DB_HOST}
EOF'), running multi-line SQL against a database, or writing multi-line JSON to curl for REST API calls. '<<-EOF' allows indentation with tabs for cleaner script formatting. Heredocs are cleaner than multiple echo statements for multi-line content generation in deployment scripts.

items.q: How do you debug a shell script?
items.hint: bash -x script.sh for trace. set -x/-e inside script. shellcheck for static analysis.
items.answer: Shell script debugging: 'bash -x script.sh' runs the script with trace mode — each command is printed with a + prefix before execution, showing variable expansions. 'set -x' inside the script enables trace from that point; 'set +x' disables it — useful to trace specific sections. 'set -e' exits on first error preventing silent failure cascades. 'set -v' prints each input line as read (before expansion). Strategic echo statements to inspect variable values. 'shellcheck script.sh' is a static analysis tool that catches common bugs — undefined variables, quoting issues, incorrect array handling — before runtime. 'trap 'echo "Error at line $LINENO"' ERR' prints the line number where errors occur, essential for debugging long scripts.

items.q: What is the significance of exit codes in shell scripting?
items.hint: 0 = success. 1-255 = failure. $? captures last code. Non-zero fails CI/CD pipeline stages.
items.answer: Exit codes (0-255) are returned by every command and script. 0 universally means success. 1-255 indicates failure. Common codes: 1=general error, 2=shell built-in misuse, 126=permission denied, 127=command not found, 130=terminated by Ctrl+C, 137=killed by SIGKILL or OOM. '$?' contains the exit code of the last command. In conditionals: 'if command; then ...' evaluates the exit code. 'command1 && command2' runs command2 only if command1 succeeds. 'command1 || command2' runs command2 only if command1 fails. In Jenkins pipelines, a non-zero exit code from a shell step fails the build stage — scripts must propagate failures correctly rather than swallowing errors with '|| true'.

items.q: How do you write a deployment shell script?
items.hint: Validate args, pull artifact, stop service, backup, deploy, verify health, rollback on failure.
items.answer: Production deployment script structure: 1. Header: set -euo pipefail and argument validation with usage function. 2. Configuration variables (DEPLOY_DIR, ARTIFACT, SERVICE_NAME). 3. Logging function writing timestamped messages to a dated log file. 4. Pre-deploy checks: verify target server reachable, disk space available, no active deployments. 5. Pull artifact from Artifactory with checksum verification. 6. Stop service with timeout. 7. Backup current deployment with timestamp. 8. Deploy new version. 9. Start service and wait for health check URL to return 200 with a loop and timeout. 10. Post-deploy smoke tests. 11. Trap on ERR to trigger rollback: 'trap rollback ERR'. Rollback function restores backup, restarts service, and alerts the on-call team. Every step logged for audit trail.

items.q: What is Terraform and what are its main benefits?
items.hint: HashiCorp IaC tool. Declarative, multi-cloud, plan before apply, state management, modular.
items.answer: Terraform is HashiCorp's open-source Infrastructure as Code tool for provisioning and managing infrastructure through declarative configuration files. Key benefits: multi-cloud support (AWS, Azure, GCP, Kubernetes via providers), declarative — you define desired end state and Terraform determines how to achieve it, 'terraform plan' shows exactly what will change before any modification, idempotent — running apply multiple times reaches the same state, modular design through reusable modules, and a rich ecosystem with thousands of community providers. Terraform enables infrastructure changes to go through the same PR review and CI/CD pipeline process as application code.

items.q: What is Terraform state and why is it important?
items.hint: terraform.tfstate maps config to real resources. Remote state in S3+DynamoDB for teams. Never commit to Git.
items.answer: Terraform state (terraform.tfstate) maps Terraform configuration to actual infrastructure resources, storing resource IDs, metadata, and dependency relationships. Terraform uses state to determine what exists, what changes need to be made, and what to destroy. Without state Terraform cannot track whether resources already exist. Local state cannot be shared and has no locking — two people running apply simultaneously could corrupt infrastructure. Solution: remote state backend. Store state in S3 with DynamoDB for locking: 'backend { type="s3", bucket="company-tf-state", key="prod/main.tfstate", dynamodb_table="tf-state-lock" }'. Never commit .tfstate files to Git — they contain sensitive values in plaintext.

items.q: What are Terraform providers and how do you configure them?
items.hint: Plugins for specific platforms (AWS, Azure, Kubernetes). Configure in required_providers and provider blocks.
items.answer: Terraform providers are plugins that know how to interact with a specific API — AWS, Azure, Kubernetes providers define available resource types. Declared in required_providers and configured in provider blocks: 'provider "aws" { region = "eu-west-2", assume_role { role_arn = "arn:aws:iam::123:role/terraform" } }'. Provider authentication uses environment variables (AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID) or IAM instance roles — never hardcode credentials in provider blocks. 'terraform init' downloads required provider versions. Version constraints ('>= 4.0, < 5.0') prevent unexpected breaking changes from automatic provider upgrades and should always be specified for production code.

items.q: What is the Terraform workflow?
items.hint: init → validate → plan → apply → destroy. Plan shows changes, apply executes, always review plan first.
items.answer: Core Terraform workflow: 'terraform init' initialises the working directory, downloads providers and modules, sets up the backend. 'terraform validate' checks syntax without contacting providers. 'terraform plan' creates an execution plan showing resources to create (+), modify (~), or destroy (-) — no actual changes made. 'terraform apply' executes the plan — prompts for confirmation unless --auto-approve. 'terraform destroy' removes all managed resources — use with extreme caution in production. In CI/CD: run plan in PR pipeline and save to file, require human approval for production, then apply using the saved plan file to guarantee the applied plan matches exactly what was reviewed.

items.q: What are Terraform modules and how do you use them?
items.hint: Reusable config bundles. Local ./modules/ or registry modules. Pass input variables, receive outputs.
items.answer: Modules are containers for multiple Terraform resources treated as a single unit — enabling reuse and abstraction. Local module: 'module "vpc" { source = "./modules/vpc", cidr_block = "10.0.0.0/16" }'. Registry module: 'module "eks" { source = "terraform-aws-modules/eks/aws", version = "~> 19.0" }'. Modules accept input variables (defined in their variables.tf) and expose outputs (defined in outputs.tf) that calling modules can reference. The same VPC module can create a dev VPC with a small CIDR and a prod VPC with a large CIDR by passing different variable values — eliminating code duplication across environments.

items.q: What is a Terraform variable and how do you define it?
items.hint: variable block with type, default, description. Set via .tfvars file, -var flag, or TF_VAR_ env var.
items.answer: Variables parameterise configurations. Defined with variable blocks: 'variable "environment" { type = string, description = "Deployment environment", default = "dev" }'. Types: string, number, bool, list(string), map(string), object. Variables without defaults are required — Terraform prompts interactively if not provided. Setting values: terraform.tfvars (auto-loaded), named .tfvars with -var-file="prod.tfvars", command line -var="environment=prod", or TF_VAR_environment environment variable — useful in CI/CD without committing values. Mark sensitive=true to prevent values appearing in plan output and logs.

items.q: How do you manage multiple environments with Terraform?
items.hint: Workspaces for simple isolation, directory-based, or Terragrunt for large-scale management.
items.answer: Multiple approaches for multi-environment management. Workspaces: 'terraform workspace new prod' creates isolated state for each workspace — same configuration, different state files. Simple but configuration complexity grows. Directory-based: separate directories per environment (environments/dev/, environments/prod/) with their own main.tf and tfvars — clearest separation but most code duplication unless modules are well-factored. Terragrunt (wrapper around Terraform): defines infrastructure in reusable modules and environment-specific HCL files calling modules with different variables, managing state paths automatically — the most scalable approach for large organisations with many environments and teams.

items.q: What is Terraform remote backend?
items.hint: Stores state remotely (S3, Azure Blob). Enables team collaboration, state locking, encryption, history.
items.answer: A remote backend stores the Terraform state file in a shared location rather than locally. AWS S3 backend: state in versioned S3 bucket with DynamoDB table for state locking — prevents simultaneous applies. Configuration: 'backend "s3" { bucket="company-terraform-state", key="services/app/terraform.tfstate", region="eu-west-2", dynamodb_table="terraform-state-lock", encrypt=true }'. Azure uses azurerm backend with Blob Storage. Terraform Cloud/Enterprise provides a managed backend with built-in locking, audit logs, run history, and policy enforcement. Benefits: team collaboration, locking preventing corruption, state encryption at rest, versioning for recovery. 'terraform init' with a defined backend will prompt to migrate existing local state.

items.q: What is the difference between terraform apply and terraform plan?
items.hint: plan: dry run, no changes. apply: executes changes. In CI/CD save plan file and apply that exact file.
items.answer: 'terraform plan' performs a dry run — reads current state, compares to desired configuration, and shows what would change. It makes no changes to real infrastructure and is safe to run at any time. 'terraform apply' executes the changes — first runs an implicit plan, displays it, asks for confirmation, then provisions or modifies resources. In CI/CD: run plan in the PR pipeline for review and save to a file ('terraform plan -out=tfplan'). In the deployment pipeline, apply using the saved plan ('terraform apply tfplan') — this guarantees the applied plan is exactly what was reviewed, with no drift between review and apply time.

items.q: How do you import existing infrastructure into Terraform?
items.hint: terraform import resource_type.name resource_id. Updates state only — must write config manually first.
items.answer: 'terraform import' brings existing infrastructure under Terraform management without recreating it. Syntax: 'terraform import aws_instance.web i-1234567890abcdef0'. This updates the state file — it does NOT generate configuration code. You must write the Terraform resource configuration manually to match the existing resource first, then run import. After import, run 'terraform plan' — ideally it shows no changes meaning your config matches reality. If changes are shown, update the config until plan is clean. 'terraform state list' shows all managed resources. 'terraform state show aws_instance.web' displays the full state for a resource — useful as reference when writing config after import.

items.q: What are Terraform data sources?
items.hint: Read-only data from existing resources. data {} block. Reference with data.type.name.attribute.
items.answer: Data sources read information about existing infrastructure not managed by this Terraform code and use it in configuration — they are read-only. Examples: 'data "aws_ami" "amazon_linux" { most_recent = true, owners = ["amazon"] }' fetches the latest Amazon Linux AMI ID. 'data "aws_vpc" "main" { tags = { Name = "production" } }' retrieves an existing VPC by tag. Reference: '${data.aws_ami.amazon_linux.id}'. Common uses: referencing existing VPCs created by another team, fetching KMS key ARNs, reading Secrets Manager values, discovering Route53 zone IDs. Data sources query the provider API at plan time so they always return the current live state of external resources.

items.q: What is terraform.tfvars and how is it used?
items.hint: Auto-loaded variable definition file. Never commit files with secrets. Use env vars for sensitive values.
items.answer: terraform.tfvars (or any *.tfvars file) defines values for declared variables. Terraform automatically loads terraform.tfvars in the working directory. Format: 'environment = "production", region = "eu-west-2", instance_count = 5'. For sensitive values use environment variables (TF_VAR_db_password) instead — never commit secrets to Git. Typical setup: terraform.tfvars contains non-sensitive defaults (region, naming conventions), environments/prod.tfvars contains production-specific non-sensitive overrides, and sensitive values come from CI/CD environment variables or Vault. Override priority (lowest to highest): default values → terraform.tfvars → named .tfvars files → TF_VAR_ variables → -var flags.

items.q: How do you handle Terraform state conflicts?
items.hint: Remote backend with locking prevents most conflicts. force-unlock if stuck. state mv/rm for manual fixes.
items.answer: State conflicts occur when two processes try to modify state simultaneously. Prevention: remote backend with state locking (DynamoDB + S3) — only one operation can run at a time. If a Terraform run crashes while holding a lock, subsequent runs fail with 'Error acquiring state lock'. Resolution: 'terraform force-unlock <lock-id>' (get lock ID from the error) — use only when certain no other apply is running. For state inconsistencies: 'terraform import' to add real resources not in state. 'terraform state rm resource.name' removes from state without destroying the real resource. 'terraform state mv' renames resources in state (useful after module refactoring without destroying real resources). Always take a backup before manual state manipulation: 'terraform state pull > backup.tfstate'.

items.q: What is Terraform workspace and when do you use it?
items.hint: Named isolated state instances within same backend. Best for identical infrastructure with variable differences.
items.answer: Terraform workspaces provide multiple named states within a single backend, allowing the same code to manage separate infrastructure instances. Commands: 'terraform workspace new staging', 'terraform workspace list', 'terraform workspace select prod'. The default workspace is named 'default'. In configuration 'terraform.workspace' returns the current workspace name — useful for conditionals: 'instance_type = terraform.workspace == "prod" ? "t3.large" : "t3.micro"'. State files are stored separately per workspace in the backend. Workspaces work best for truly identical infrastructure with only variable differences. For environments with significantly different configurations, separate root modules per environment provide clearer separation and are easier to manage independently.

items.q: How do you use Terraform with CI/CD pipelines?
items.hint: init → validate → plan (PR review) → approved apply (on merge). Remote state, Vault for secrets, approval gates.
items.answer: Terraform CI/CD best practices: use a dedicated CI service account with minimum required permissions. Store state in remote backend (S3 + DynamoDB). On pull request: run 'terraform init', 'terraform validate', 'terraform fmt --check', 'terraform plan' and post the plan output as a PR comment — tools like Atlantis or custom GitHub Actions automate this. Never auto-apply from feature branches. On merge to main: run 'terraform apply' using the exact plan file from the PR pipeline (prevents plan-to-apply drift).
