Implement approval gates for production requiring explicit human sign-off. Manage secrets via Vault or environment variables — never in state or code. Run 'tflint' in pre-commit hooks for early feedback before CI runs.

items.q: What is Terraform resource dependency and how does it work?
items.hint: Implicit: reference output of resource A in resource B. Explicit: depends_on. Graph-based execution order.
items.answer: Terraform determines resource creation order through a dependency graph. Implicit dependencies are created when one resource references an attribute of another: 'subnet_id = aws_subnet.main.id' — Terraform knows the subnet must exist before the EC2 instance. The dependency graph ensures resources are created in correct order and destroyed in reverse. Explicit dependencies using depends_on are needed when resources have a dependency not reflected in their configuration attributes — for example an EC2 instance requiring an IAM role policy to exist but not referencing it directly. 'terraform graph' outputs the dependency graph in DOT format for visualisation. Circular dependencies cause 'Cycle error' and must be resolved by restructuring resources.

items.q: What is Microsoft Azure and what are the core services you have worked with?
items.hint: Microsoft cloud platform. VMs, VNet, AKS, Storage, Azure DevOps, Active Directory, App Service.
items.answer: Microsoft Azure is Microsoft's cloud computing platform offering IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS services. Core services I have worked with: Azure Virtual Machines for compute, Azure Virtual Network (VNet) for networking, Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS) for managed Kubernetes, Azure Blob Storage for object storage, Azure DevOps for CI/CD pipelines, Azure Active Directory (Entra ID) for identity management, Azure App Service for web application hosting, Azure SQL Database for managed relational databases, Azure Monitor with Log Analytics for observability, and Azure Container Registry (ACR) for Docker images. Azure's tight Microsoft ecosystem integration makes it the default cloud choice for organisations already using Active Directory and Office 365.

items.q: What is an Azure Resource Group and how is it used?
items.hint: Logical container for Azure resources. Lifecycle management, RBAC scope, billing boundary.
items.answer: An Azure Resource Group is a logical container holding related Azure resources for an application or workload. Resources in a group share the same lifecycle — you can deploy, update, monitor, and delete them together. Resource groups define the scope for RBAC — granting a team Contributor role on a resource group gives access to all resources within it. They also provide a cost management boundary in Azure Cost Management. Resources can communicate across resource groups, but keeping application components together simplifies operational management. Naming convention: {company}-{environment}-{application}-rg (e.g. acme-prod-webapp-rg) makes resource groups self-documenting.

items.q: What is Azure DevOps and how does it compare to Jenkins?
items.hint: Microsoft's integrated DevOps platform. Pipelines (YAML), Repos (Git), Boards, Artifacts. Cloud-managed, no servers.
items.answer: Azure DevOps provides: Azure Pipelines (CI/CD with YAML or GUI), Azure Repos (Git source control), Azure Boards (Agile project management), Azure Artifacts (package management), and Azure Test Plans. Unlike Jenkins which requires plugin management and server maintenance, Azure DevOps is fully managed SaaS — no servers to maintain. Azure Pipelines supports Microsoft-hosted agents (Windows, Linux, Mac) and self-hosted agents for internal network access. YAML pipelines are stored in the repo as code with multi-stage support. Integration with Azure services (AKS, ACR, App Service) is native. For .NET teams Azure DevOps provides the tightest integration; for polyglot environments Jenkins or GitHub Actions may offer more flexibility.

items.q: What is Azure AKS and how does it relate to OpenShift?
items.hint: Managed Kubernetes on Azure. Microsoft manages control plane. ARO is OpenShift managed on Azure.
items.answer: Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS) is Microsoft's managed Kubernetes — Azure manages the control plane at no charge, you pay only for worker nodes. AKS integrates natively with Azure AD for pod-level authentication (Workload Identity), Azure Container Registry, Azure Monitor Container Insights, Azure Disk/Files for persistent volumes, and Azure Load Balancer. Red Hat OpenShift on Azure (ARO) is a fully managed OpenShift service jointly supported by Microsoft and Red Hat — it provides OpenShift enterprise features (OperatorHub, Routes, SCCs, built-in monitoring) on Azure infrastructure. ARO is preferred in enterprises already using OpenShift on-premises wanting cloud scalability; AKS is preferred for teams starting fresh on Azure without prior OpenShift investment.

items.q: What is Azure Monitor and how do you use it?
items.hint: Metrics, logs (Log Analytics with KQL), alerts, Application Insights, dashboards and Workbooks.
items.answer: Azure Monitor is the unified monitoring platform. Key components: Metrics — time-series data from Azure resources stored for 93 days. Log Analytics Workspace — centralised log aggregation using KQL (Kusto Query Language) for querying logs from VMs, AKS, App Service, and custom applications. Application Insights — APM for application-level monitoring with distributed tracing, dependency mapping, live metrics, and exception tracking. Alerts — triggered by metric thresholds, log query results, or resource health — notify via email, SMS, webhook, or PagerDuty. In practice: enable Azure Monitor for Containers on AKS for cluster metrics, send application logs to Log Analytics, create alert rules for critical metrics, and build Workbooks for operations team dashboards combining multiple data sources.

items.q: What is Azure Virtual Network and how do you configure it?
items.hint: Isolated network. Subnets, NSGs (stateful firewall), peering, VPN Gateway, private endpoints.
items.answer: Azure Virtual Network (VNet) provides isolated private networking. You define an address space (e.g. 10.0.0.0/16) and divide into subnets (web tier, app tier, data tier). Network Security Groups (NSGs) are stateful firewalls attached to subnets or NICs — define inbound and outbound rules with priority numbers. VNet Peering connects two VNets within the same or different regions. VPN Gateway or ExpressRoute connects to on-premises networks. Private Endpoints provide private IP access to PaaS services (Azure SQL, Storage, KeyVault) within the VNet, eliminating public internet exposure for sensitive data services. Service endpoints provide optimised routing from VNet to Azure PaaS services without private IPs, as a lighter alternative.

items.q: What is Azure Active Directory?
items.hint: Cloud identity platform. SSO, MFA, Conditional Access, RBAC for Azure, App Registrations for OAuth2/OIDC.
items.answer: Azure Active Directory (now Microsoft Entra ID) is Microsoft's cloud identity and access management platform providing authentication for Microsoft 365, Azure services, and thousands of SaaS applications. Key features: SSO across all integrated applications, MFA for enhanced security, Conditional Access policies controlling access based on user, device, location, and risk signals, RBAC for Azure resources (assign roles to users or groups), and App Registrations for OAuth 2.0/OIDC integration. In enterprises AAD is synchronised with on-premises Active Directory via Azure AD Connect for hybrid identity. For DevOps, AAD provides service principals for CI/CD pipelines to authenticate to Azure without user credentials — assign minimum required RBAC roles to service principals.

items.q: What is Azure Blob Storage and when do you use it?
items.hint: Object storage for files, logs, backups, Terraform state. Access tiers: Hot, Cool, Archive.
items.answer: Azure Blob Storage is Azure's object storage for unstructured data — files, images, logs, backups, VM disk images, and Terraform state files. Three access tiers: Hot (frequently accessed — lowest access cost, highest storage cost), Cool (infrequently accessed, minimum 30 days — lower storage cost), and Archive (minimum 180 days — lowest storage cost, high retrieval latency). Security: configure private endpoints to block public internet access, use SAS (Shared Access Signatures) for time-limited access, enable soft delete and versioning for accidental deletion protection. Lifecycle management policies automatically tier or delete blobs based on age — essential for cost-controlling CI/CD artifact storage and application log archives.

items.q: How do you implement CI/CD with Azure Pipelines?
items.hint: azure-pipelines.yml in repo root. Stages with environments and approval gates. Service connections for external systems.
items.answer: Azure Pipelines uses azure-pipelines.yml at the repo root. Multi-stage pipeline: trigger (branch filter), pool (agent), variables, and stages. Example: Build stage compiles and pushes container image to ACR. Deploy-Dev stage auto-deploys to AKS dev namespace. Deploy-Prod stage has an environment approval gate requiring designated approver sign-off before running. Service connections in Azure DevOps provide authenticated connections to Azure subscriptions (via service principal), Docker registries, and Kubernetes clusters. Variable groups in Library store environment-specific variables backed by Azure Key Vault for secrets. YAML templates provide reusable stage and step definitions shared across pipelines — equivalent to Jenkins shared libraries.

items.q: What is Azure Key Vault and how do you use it in applications?
items.hint: Managed secrets, keys, certificates. Application retrieves via Managed Identity. Never hardcode secrets.
items.answer: Azure Key Vault stores and manages secrets (passwords, API keys), encryption keys, and TLS certificates. Applications access secrets at runtime using Managed Identity — assign the 'Key Vault Secrets User' role to the application's identity, then use the Azure SDK. Key Vault integrates with Azure App Service, AKS (via CSI Secret Store driver or External Secrets Operator), Azure DevOps (variable groups backed by Key Vault), and Terraform. Features: soft delete and purge protection prevents accidental permanent deletion, RBAC controls who can manage vs use secrets, audit logs in Azure Monitor record every secret access — critical for compliance in regulated industries. Rotate secrets with automatic rotation policies for supported services like Azure SQL database passwords.

items.q: What is the difference between Azure IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS?
items.hint: IaaS: VMs (you manage OS+). PaaS: App Service, AKS (you manage app). SaaS: Office 365 (Microsoft manages all).
items.answer: IaaS (Infrastructure as a Service): Azure provides virtualised compute, storage, and networking — you manage the OS, runtime, application, and data. Examples: Azure Virtual Machines, Azure Disk Storage. Maximum control, maximum responsibility. PaaS (Platform as a Service): Azure manages infrastructure and runtime — you deploy and manage application code and data. Examples: Azure App Service, Azure Kubernetes Service (Azure manages control plane), Azure SQL Database. Faster deployment, less maintenance overhead. SaaS (Software as a Service): the entire application is managed by Microsoft — you use it via browser. Examples: Microsoft 365, Dynamics 365. The shared responsibility model determines what each party manages for security — affecting compliance obligations particularly in regulated industries.

items.q: How do you configure auto-scaling in Azure?
items.hint: VMSS with autoscale rules. AKS: HPA for pods, Cluster Autoscaler for nodes. Azure Monitor metrics trigger scaling.
items.answer: Azure provides auto-scaling at multiple levels. VM Scale Sets (VMSS): define min/max/default instance count with scale-out rules (add instances when CPU > 70% for 5 minutes) and scale-in rules (remove instances when CPU < 30%). AKS: Horizontal Pod Autoscaler (HPA) adjusts pod replicas based on CPU, memory, or custom metrics (KEDA for event-driven scaling). Cluster Autoscaler adds or removes AKS node pools when pods are pending or nodes are underutilised. Azure App Service: scale out by instance count based on HTTP queue length or schedule. Combine HPA with KEDA in AKS for event-driven scaling based on Azure Service Bus queue depth, enabling scale-to-zero for batch workloads and eliminating idle compute costs.

items.q: What is Azure ExpressRoute?
items.hint: Dedicated private connection from on-premises to Azure. Not over public internet. Low latency, high bandwidth, SLA.
items.answer: Azure ExpressRoute provides a dedicated private network connection between on-premises infrastructure and Azure data centres, bypassing the public internet entirely. The connection is established through connectivity providers at colocation facilities. Benefits over VPN: dedicated bandwidth (50Mbps to 100Gbps), consistent low latency, higher security (traffic never traverses public internet), and 99.95% SLA. ExpressRoute circuits connect to Azure VNets via ExpressRoute Gateways. In banking environments ExpressRoute is mandatory for core system integration with Azure — regulatory requirements often prohibit routing sensitive financial data over public internet. Cost is significantly higher than VPN — used for production workloads requiring guaranteed performance and meeting strict data sovereignty requirements.

items.q: What is IIS (Internet Information Services) and what is it used for?
items.hint: Microsoft web server. Hosts ASP.NET apps, static content, HTTPS. Managed via App Pools.
items.answer: IIS (Internet Information Services) is Microsoft's web server built into Windows Server. It hosts ASP.NET and ASP.NET Core web applications, serves static content, and acts as a reverse proxy. Key components: Sites (virtual websites listening on specific IP/port/hostname), Application Pools (isolated worker processes — crash isolation prevents one app from taking down others), Virtual Directories (mapping URL paths to filesystem paths), and Bindings (HTTP/HTTPS on specific ports with SSL certificate assignments). In enterprise .NET environments IIS is the primary hosting platform for internal web applications and APIs. Modern ASP.NET Core applications can also self-host using Kestrel or run behind IIS using in-process hosting.

items.q: What is an IIS Application Pool and why is it important?
items.hint: Isolated worker process (w3wp.exe) per pool. Crash isolation, identity, .NET version, recycling.
items.answer: An IIS Application Pool is an isolated worker process (w3wp.exe) hosting one or more web applications. Isolation means if one application crashes only its pool is affected — other applications continue running. Each pool runs under a specific identity (ApplicationPoolIdentity, Network Service, or a custom service account) controlling resource access. The .NET CLR version and pipeline mode are configured per pool — essential for hosting apps requiring different .NET versions on the same IIS instance. Pool recycling automatically restarts the worker process on a schedule or based on memory and CPU thresholds, preventing memory leaks from building up. In production monitor pool health via IIS logs and Windows Event Viewer, and configure rapid-fail protection to prevent continuous crash-restart loops.

items.q: How do you configure HTTPS in IIS?
items.hint: Import certificate to Windows Certificate Store. Add HTTPS binding on 443 with certificate. Redirect HTTP to HTTPS.
items.answer: Setting up HTTPS in IIS: Import the SSL certificate into Windows Certificate Store (certlm.msc, Personal store). In IIS Manager select the site, click Bindings, Add, select HTTPS, port 443, enter hostname (SNI for multiple SSL sites on one IP), and select the certificate. Redirect HTTP to HTTPS using IIS URL Rewrite module — add a rule to match all HTTP requests and redirect to the HTTPS equivalent. Configure SSL settings: disable SSL 3.0 and TLS 1.0/1.1 using IIS Crypto tool, enable only TLS 1.2/1.3. Configure HSTS headers using IIS Custom Response Headers. Automate certificate renewal with ACME clients for Let's Encrypt or use your enterprise PKI/CA for internal certificates, integrating renewal into a scheduled task or pipeline.

items.q: How do you troubleshoot a 500 error in IIS?
items.hint: Enable detailed errors in web.config. Check Event Log, IIS logs in C:\inetpub\logs. Use Failed Request Tracing.
items.answer: IIS 500 error troubleshooting: Enable detailed error messages by adding httpErrors errorMode="Detailed" in system.webServer section of web.config. IIS logs in C:\inetpub\logs\LogFiles show sc-status and sc-substatus — 500.19 means config error, 500.21 means module error, 500.30 means ASP.NET app startup failure. Windows Application Event Log (eventvwr.msc) shows ASP.NET application errors with full stack traces. Enable Failed Request Tracing (FREB) in IIS Manager — it logs detailed request processing steps and the exact module where processing failed. Check app pool identity has read access to application files. Verify the correct .NET Framework or .NET Core hosting bundle is installed and matches the application's target framework.

items.q: What is Windows Task Scheduler and how do you use it for automation?
items.hint: Schedule scripts at specific times or events. GUI (taskschd.msc) or schtasks command line.
items.answer: Windows Task Scheduler automates running scripts, programs, or batch files based on time schedules or system events. Tasks have a trigger (daily, at startup, on logon, on event log entry) and an action (run program). Command line creation: 'schtasks /create /tn "Backup" /tr "C:\scripts\backup.ps1" /sc DAILY /st 02:00 /ru SYSTEM'. Critical settings: run under a service account with minimum required permissions, check 'Run whether user is logged on or not', set 'Stop the task if it runs longer than' to prevent hung tasks, and log output to files for debugging. In DevOps Task Scheduler handles automated backups, certificate renewals, cleanup scripts, and health checks on Windows servers where systemd is not available.

items.q: What is PowerShell and how do you use it for DevOps tasks?
items.hint: Microsoft scripting shell. Manage Windows, Azure, IIS, AD. Remoting, pipelines, modules.
items.answer: PowerShell is Microsoft's cross-platform scripting language built on .NET. It excels at managing Windows infrastructure: IIS (WebAdministration module: 'Import-Module WebAdministration; Get-WebSite'), Windows Services ('Get-Service | Where Status -eq Running'), Active Directory (Get-ADUser), File operations, Registry, and Event Logs. PowerShell Remoting ('Invoke-Command -ComputerName server01 -ScriptBlock { Get-Service }') runs commands on remote servers — essential for managing server farms. Azure PowerShell manages Azure resources via the Az module. For DevOps: script IIS deployments, automate certificate management, manage Windows services (Start-Service, Stop-Service), and configure Windows Firewall. PowerShell DSC (Desired State Configuration) is an IaC approach for Windows server configuration management.

items.q: What are Windows Event Logs and how do you use them for troubleshooting?
items.hint: Application, System, Security logs. Event Viewer GUI or Get-EventLog PowerShell. Common EventIDs for issues.
items.answer: Windows Event Logs record system, application, and security events. Three main logs: Application (application errors, IIS logs, .NET exceptions — most useful for application troubleshooting), System (Windows service failures, driver issues, hardware errors), Security (login attempts, privilege use, account management). Access via Event Viewer (eventvwr.msc) or PowerShell: 'Get-EventLog -LogName Application -EntryType Error -Newest 50'. Common Event IDs: 1000/1001 (application crash), 4625 (failed login), 7034/7036 (service crashed/stopped). In production monitoring forward Windows Event Logs to a centralised SIEM (Splunk, Elastic SIEM) or Azure Monitor using the Windows Event Log connector for centralised analysis and alerting across the server fleet.

items.q: What is Active Directory and how does it work?
items.hint: Microsoft directory service. Domains, OUs, users, groups, GPOs, LDAP auth. Domain Controllers store user database.
items.answer: Active Directory (AD) is Microsoft's directory service for Windows domain networks — it stores and manages identities (users, computers, groups) and enforces access policies. Structure: Forest (top-level security boundary), Domains (administrative units), OUs (containers for organising objects). Domain Controllers host the AD database and authenticate logon requests. LDAP is the communication protocol for querying AD. Group Policy Objects (GPOs) enforce configuration and security settings — software installation, security settings, mapped drives, firewall rules. In enterprise Java applications WebSphere and Tomcat integrate with AD via LDAP for user authentication. AD service accounts run application services with controlled permissions and centralised password management in enterprise environments.

items.q: How do you manage Windows services?
items.hint: services.msc GUI, sc.exe command line, PowerShell Get/Start/Stop/Restart-Service. Recovery options for auto-restart.
items.answer: Windows services are long-running background processes. Management methods: services.msc GUI provides visual status and configuration. sc.exe command line: 'sc query tomcat', 'sc start tomcat', 'sc stop tomcat', 'sc config tomcat start= auto'. PowerShell: 'Get-Service -Name Tomcat9', 'Start-Service Tomcat9', 'Restart-Service Tomcat9', 'Set-Service -Name Tomcat9 -StartupType Automatic'. Service recovery settings (Properties → Recovery tab): configure First/Second failure actions — set to 'Restart the Service' with a restart delay for production services to recover automatically from crashes without operator intervention. Run production services under dedicated domain service accounts with minimum required permissions rather than Local System which has full local admin rights.

items.q: What is Windows IIS URL Rewrite and how do you use it?
items.hint: URL Rewrite module for redirect/rewrite rules. HTTP to HTTPS, canonical URLs, reverse proxy in web.config.
items.answer: IIS URL Rewrite is a separately installed module that modifies incoming request URLs based on rules configured in web.config within the system.webServer/rewrite section. Common use cases: HTTP to HTTPS redirect — match condition checks HTTPS off, action type Redirect to https://{HTTP_HOST}/{R:1}. Canonical URL enforcement — redirect www to non-www. Reverse proxy rules — rewrite requests from /api/* to a backend service using Application Request Routing (ARR) module. Rule components: match (URL pattern using regex), conditions (server variables like HTTPS, HTTP_HOST), action (Redirect with 301/302, Rewrite, Custom Response). Store rules in web.config for version control and consistent deployment across environments in a web farm.

items.q: How do you monitor IIS performance?
items.hint: Performance Monitor (perfmon), IIS logs analysis, Application Insights, ARR counters. Monitor requests/sec, 5xx rates, response times.
items.answer: IIS performance monitoring: Windows Performance Monitor (perfmon.msc) provides IIS counters — W3SVC_W3WP Active Requests, ASP.NET Applications Requests/Sec, ASP.NET Request Wait Time, Memory Available MBytes. IIS access logs in C:\inetpub\logs record every request with status codes and time-taken — analyse with Log Parser or PowerShell to calculate error rates and find slow requests. Application Insights provides APM for IIS-hosted apps with auto-instrumentation, request rates, response times, and exception rates. Failed Request Tracing (FREB) diagnoses intermittent slow requests by logging detailed processing steps. Set up Windows Performance Alerts in perfmon to notify when key metrics exceed thresholds such as thread queue length or available memory falling below a safe threshold.

items.q: What is Windows Server Failover Clustering?
items.hint: HA for Windows services. Multiple nodes, shared storage, heartbeat, automatic failover on node failure.
items.answer: Windows Server Failover Clustering (WSFC) provides high availability for Windows services. Multiple server nodes (minimum 2) form a cluster sharing a quorum disk (or cloud witness for Azure) that determines cluster health. Cluster resources — services, IP addresses, file shares — are grouped into Cluster Roles that automatically fail over to a surviving node when the active node fails. Common applications: SQL Server Always On Availability Groups for database HA, File Server for shared storage HA. All cluster nodes need identical hardware, the same Windows Server version, and shared storage (SAN LUNs or Cluster Shared Volumes). Quorum configuration prevents split-brain scenarios — determines how many votes are needed for the cluster to remain operational when network partitions occur.

items.q: How do you deploy a .NET application to IIS via CI/CD?
items.hint: MSDeploy/Web Deploy, PowerShell Remoting for xcopy deployment. Stop app pool, deploy, restart, verify.
items.answer: Automated IIS deployment in CI/CD: Web Deploy (MSDeploy) is the standard approach — publish a web deploy package and push it to IIS with 'msdeploy.exe -verb:sync -source:package=myapp.zip -dest:auto,computerName=targetserver'. Requires Web Management Service on the target. Alternatively use PowerShell Remoting with robocopy: stop app pool ('Invoke-Command -ComputerName server -ScriptBlock { Stop-WebAppPool -Name MyPool }'), copy files, restart app pool. In Azure DevOps use the IIS Web App Deploy task. Rolling deployments in a web farm: drain and remove one node from load balancer, deploy, verify health, re-add, then proceed to the next node. Always back up before deployment: 'Compress-Archive -Path $webroot -DestinationPath backup_$(Get-Date -f yyyyMMdd).zip'.

items.q: What is Bitbucket and how does it fit in a CI/CD pipeline?
items.hint: Atlassian Git hosting. Webhooks trigger Jenkins. Branch permissions enforce code review before merge.
items.answer: Bitbucket is Atlassian's Git repository hosting platform (cloud and self-hosted Data Center). In a CI/CD pipeline it is the source of truth for application code and Jenkinsfiles. Developers push code to Bitbucket which fires a webhook to Jenkins triggering the pipeline automatically. Bitbucket provides PR workflows, branch permissions, and code review enforcement. In my setup the main branch was protected — all changes required a PR approved by at least one reviewer, and the Jenkins pipeline had to pass on the feature branch before merging was allowed, enforcing quality gates at the source control level.

items.q: What is a pull request and how do you enforce code review in Bitbucket?
items.hint: Branch permissions: require approvals, restrict direct push to main, require CI pass.
items.answer: A pull request (PR) is a proposal to merge code from one branch into another with a review interface for comments and approvals. In Bitbucket, branch permissions are configured under Repository Settings to enforce PR workflows on protected branches. You can require a minimum number of approvals, prevent the author from approving their own PR, require all build status checks to pass (CI pipeline), and block direct force pushes. In banking we required 2 approvals for production branches and 1 for development branches, with mandatory CI pass — every merge to main triggered a full build and test cycle before acceptance, maintaining code quality standards consistently.

items.q: What is Bitbucket branching strategy and which one have you used?
items.hint: Gitflow: main, develop, feature/, hotfix/. Trunk-based: short-lived branches off main. Banking uses Gitflow variant.
items.answer: The two main strategies are Gitflow and trunk-based development. Gitflow uses long-lived branches — main (production), develop (integration), feature/* (new work), release/* (release preparation), hotfix/* (emergency fixes) — suitable for scheduled releases. Trunk-based development keeps a single main branch with short-lived feature branches merged frequently, enabling true continuous integration. In my banking project I used a simplified Gitflow: feature branches off develop, merged to develop for integration testing, then promoted to main for production releases via a scheduled release process with change management approval — balancing development speed with the change control requirements of a regulated environment.

items.q: How do you handle merge conflicts in Git/Bitbucket?
items.hint: Rebase or merge locally, resolve conflicts marked by Git, push resolved branch.
items.answer: Merge conflicts occur when two branches modify the same lines differently. Resolution process: fetch the latest target branch, rebase or merge it into your feature branch locally, open conflicting files where Git marks sections with <<<<, ====, and >>>> markers, manually edit to keep the correct code, mark as resolved with git add, complete the merge/rebase, and push. In a team environment frequent rebasing off the main branch reduces conflict severity. For complex conflicts on shared files like pom.xml or Jenkinsfiles I always discuss with the other developer before resolving to ensure business logic is not accidentally lost in the merge resolution.

items.q: What are Bitbucket pipelines and how do they differ from Jenkins?
items.hint: Built-in CI/CD in Bitbucket Cloud using bitbucket-pipelines.yml. Simpler but less powerful than Jenkins.
items.answer: Bitbucket Pipelines is a built-in CI/CD service in Bitbucket Cloud configured via a bitbucket-pipelines.yml file. It uses Docker containers for each step and does not require a separate Jenkins server — simpler setup for small teams. Limitations: execution minutes are capped per plan, customisation is more limited, and self-hosted runners require additional setup. Jenkins is more powerful for complex enterprise pipelines with extensive plugin ecosystems, shared libraries, and integration with on-premise infrastructure. I have used Jenkins extensively in banking where we had on-premise infrastructure, needed fine-grained control over build environments, and required integration with internal systems not reachable from Bitbucket Cloud's hosted runners.

items.q: How do you configure webhooks between Bitbucket and Jenkins?
items.hint: Bitbucket repo Settings → Webhooks → add Jenkins URL /bitbucket-hook/ with push and PR events.
items.answer: In Bitbucket go to Repository Settings → Webhooks → Add webhook. Enter the Jenkins URL in the format https://jenkins-server/bitbucket-hook/ (for the Bitbucket plugin) and select trigger events — repository push and pull request created/updated. On the Jenkins side install the Bitbucket plugin and enable 'Build when a change is pushed to Bitbucket' in the job configuration. For multibranch pipelines the plugin automatically handles branch and PR discovery. Ensure Jenkins is network-accessible from Bitbucket — if Jenkins is internal, a reverse proxy or using Bitbucket Data Center on the same internal network is needed. Test webhooks using Bitbucket's built-in webhook tester to confirm delivery and Jenkins response.

items.q: What is git rebase vs git merge?
items.hint: Merge preserves history with merge commit. Rebase rewrites history for linear commit log. Never rebase public branches.
items.answer: Git merge combines branches by creating a new merge commit with two parents — the commit graph shows a fork and rejoin, preserving complete history of both branches. Git rebase moves your branch commits on top of the target branch, creating new commits with the same changes but rewritten history — the result is a linear commit log. Rebase creates cleaner history but rewrites commit SHAs, so you must never rebase public or shared branches as it causes problems for others who have pulled that history. In practice: use merge for integrating completed features to maintain history, use rebase for keeping your feature branch up to date with main during development to stay current with the latest changes.

items.q: How do you tag releases in Git/Bitbucket?
items.hint: git tag -a v1.0.0 -m message. Push with git push origin --tags. Automate tagging in Jenkins on successful deploy.
items.answer: Git tags mark specific commits as important reference points, typically used for releases. Annotated tags ('git tag -a v1.0.0 -m "Production release 1.0.0"') include a message, tagger name, and date — preferred for releases. Push tags separately with 'git push origin --tags' or 'git push origin v1.0.0' for a specific tag. In Jenkins pipelines I automate tagging on successful production deployments — the pipeline creates a tag with the version number after a successful release using 'git tag' and 'git push'. Tags provide immutable reference points allowing you to check out the exact code deployed to production at any time for debugging or audit purposes.

items.q: What is .gitignore and what should you put in it?
items.hint: Exclude build outputs, IDE files, secrets, logs from version control. Templates at gitignore.io.
items.answer: .gitignore tells Git which files and directories to exclude from version control. It should contain: build output directories (target/, build/, dist/), IDE configuration files (.idea/, *.iml), OS files (.DS_Store, Thumbs.db), log files (*.log), dependency directories (node_modules/), and critically — any files containing secrets, passwords, or environment-specific configuration (*.env, application-prod.properties). Templates for every language and framework are available at gitignore.io. Never commit credentials even accidentally — if you do, assume they are compromised and rotate them immediately since Git history cannot be fully purged from all clones that already exist across the team.

items.q: What is cherry-pick in Git?
items.hint: Apply a specific commit from one branch to another without merging the whole branch.
items.answer: Git cherry-pick applies a specific commit from one branch to another without merging the entire source branch. Syntax: 'git cherry-pick <commit-sha>'. Useful for backporting a bug fix from main to a release branch — cherry-pick just the fix commit rather than merging all of main. It creates a new commit on the target branch with the same changes but a new SHA. Cherry-pick should be used sparingly as it duplicates commits and can create merge confusion later. In release management at the bank I used cherry-pick for emergency hotfixes that needed to go to both production main and the develop branch simultaneously, allowing each branch to get the fix with its own commit reference.

items.q: How do you revert a bad commit in Git?
items.hint: git revert creates a new undo commit (safe for shared branches). git reset rewrites history (local branches only).
items.answer: For shared public branches use 'git revert <commit-sha>' which creates a new commit that undoes the changes — history is preserved and no force push is needed. For local or private branches 'git reset --hard <commit-sha>' moves the branch pointer back and discards commits entirely, but this rewrites history. In a Jenkins pipeline with automated deployments, a bad production deployment is best handled by triggering a redeployment of the previous known-good image tag (helm rollback) while simultaneously doing a git revert in a PR to maintain accurate history of what was deployed. Never use git reset on branches shared with the team — it causes history divergence that is painful to resolve.

items.q: What is a Git hook and have you used any?
items.hint: Scripts triggered on Git events. Pre-commit for linting, pre-push for tests, commit-msg for format checking.
items.answer: Git hooks are scripts that run automatically when specific Git events occur. Client-side hooks: pre-commit (runs before a commit — used for linting, secret scanning), commit-msg (validates commit message format), pre-push (runs tests before pushing). Server-side hooks: pre-receive (validates pushes server-side — branch permission enforcement), post-receive (triggers CI systems). I have used pre-commit hooks with the pre-commit framework to run Python linting, YAML validation, and secret detection (detect-secrets) before commits complete. This catches issues before they reach the CI pipeline, saving build time and preventing common mistakes like accidentally committed credentials or badly formatted configuration files.

items.q: Production deployment failed and the application is down. Walk me through your response.
items.hint: Immediate rollback first. Notify stakeholders. Collect evidence. RCA after restoration.
items.answer: Immediate priority is restoring service — analysis comes after. First 5 minutes: confirm the outage by checking monitoring dashboards and health check endpoints. Notify the incident channel immediately with impact scope. Execute rollback instantly — 'helm rollback myapp' for container deployments, or re-deploy the previous artifact for VM deployments. Monitor that rollback completes and health checks pass. Notify stakeholders that service is restored. Next 30 minutes: collect evidence — pipeline logs, application logs from the failure window, monitoring dashboards showing the timeline. Implement a change freeze — no further deployments until root cause is understood. Post-incident: document timeline, identify root cause, implement fix and prevention measures, update runbooks. Present findings in a blameless post-mortem within 24-48 hours.

items.q: You notice a Java application on WebSphere is consuming 100% CPU. What do you do?
items.hint: Thread dump to identify stuck threads or GC overhead. Isolate node from LB. Engage dev team with evidence.
items.answer: Step 1: take multiple thread dumps 30 seconds apart — 'kill -3 <PID>' or via admin console. Multiple dumps show whether threads are stuck (same stack trace) or actively running. Step 2: check if CPU is in GC — GC verbose logs or thread dump output showing GC threads consuming high CPU indicates memory pressure requiring heap increase. Step 3: check for infinite loops — a single thread consistently at the top of the stack in application code suggests an infinite loop. Step 4: check for lock contention — many threads blocked on the same monitor indicate a synchronisation bottleneck. Step 5: if root cause is external (database down causing connection wait threads to accumulate), address the upstream issue. Document all findings and engage the development team with concrete thread dump evidence before implementing any fix.

items.q: A Jenkins pipeline is consistently failing at the Docker build stage. How do you debug it?
items.hint: Check Dockerfile, build context, registry credentials, disk space on agent, base image availability.
items.answer: Systematic Docker build failure debugging: examine the pipeline log carefully — the actual error message is the key. Common failures: 'COPY failed: file not found' — check files referenced in Dockerfile exist in the build context and .dockerignore is not excluding them. 'Error pulling base image' — verify the base image name and tag, check the agent can reach the registry (network, proxy settings, registry credentials in Jenkins). 'No space left on device' — Docker build layers fill disk; run 'docker system prune' on the agent or increase agent disk. Authentication failures for private registries — verify Jenkins credentials are current and the registry URL is correct. 'exec format error' — building AMD64 image on ARM host, add --platform linux/amd64. Add 'docker info' as a diagnostic step before the build to confirm Docker daemon status on the agent.

items.q: Your OpenShift pods are in CrashLoopBackOff. Walk me through troubleshooting.
items.hint: oc logs --previous, oc describe pod for events, check resource limits, liveness probe settings.
items.answer: CrashLoopBackOff means a container starts, crashes, and Kubernetes backs off restart attempts. Diagnosis: 'oc logs <pod> --previous' shows logs from the last crashed instance — usually reveals the crash reason directly (application exception, missing config, failed database connection). 'oc describe pod <pod>' shows the Events section — look for OOMKilled (out of memory — increase memory limit), ImagePullBackOff (image unavailable), Liveness probe failed (misconfigured threshold killing healthy pods), or volume mount errors. Check the container exit code — exit 1 means application error, exit 137 means OOM killed. If liveness probe is misconfigured (initialDelaySeconds too short for a slow-starting app), add a startupProbe. For config issues (missing environment variables, wrong database URL), review ConfigMap and Secret bindings in the deployment spec.

items.q: How do you handle a situation where Ansible playbook fails midway through deployment?
items.hint: Idempotent plays: re-run safely. --limit failed_hosts. Rollback play for critical changes.
items.answer: Ansible failure recovery: identify which task failed from the output. If the playbook is idempotent, fix the root cause and re-run — already-completed tasks show 'ok' (no change) and execution resumes effectively. Use '--limit failed_hosts' to re-run only against hosts that failed. For non-idempotent operations (e.g. a partial database migration), assess the state manually by SSH-ing in and verifying what was applied. In Ansible Tower the failed job shows exactly which tasks passed and failed — correct the issue and relaunch. For critical production changes design playbooks with a rollback play that undoes changes — run it against affected hosts if the main play fails. Use block/rescue/always in task groups for built-in error handling and automatic cleanup on failure.

items.q: Development team says 'it works on my machine but not in production'. How do you investigate?
items.hint: Compare environment: config, secrets, dependencies, network access, resource limits. Reproduce in staging first.
items.answer: 'Works on my machine' usually means an environment configuration difference. Systematic comparison: Configuration — compare application configuration between environments (database URLs, API endpoints, feature flags). What environment variables or config files differ? Dependency versions — is the same application version, JVM version, OS version, and library version deployed? Check for hardcoded localhost references. Network connectivity — can the production application reach all required services? Test with 'curl' from the production server. Resource constraints — does production have memory or CPU limits not present locally causing OOM or throttling? Compare application startup logs between environments for configuration loading differences. Reproduce in staging with production-equivalent configuration before modifying production — never debug directly in production if avoidable.

items.q: You need to deploy a critical hotfix to production outside the normal change window. What is your process?
items.hint: Emergency change process: verbal approval, targeted testing, change freeze after, full RCA.
items.answer: Emergency change process: First verify the fix is genuinely necessary and the risk of deploying outweighs the risk of leaving the issue in production. Obtain verbal or emergency approval from the change manager and relevant stakeholders — document immediately via email or incident ticket. Apply the fix to the lowest available environment first and execute a targeted smoke test specific to the defect — even 10 minutes of testing is better than none. Brief the on-call team so they are ready to assist if deployment causes issues. Minimise scope — deploy only the specific fix, not any other pending changes. Execute with full monitoring attention. Define a clear rollback trigger threshold upfront. After successful deployment implement a change freeze for that component. Raise a full retrospective change review and root cause analysis within 24 hours.

items.q: How do you set up monitoring and alerting for a new application in production?
items.hint: Golden signals: latency, traffic, errors, saturation. Define SLIs/SLOs. Alert on symptoms. PagerDuty integration.
items.answer: Monitoring follows the four Golden Signals: Latency (95th and 99th percentile response time, not just average), Traffic (requests per second to establish normal baseline), Errors (4xx/5xx rate — alert when above 0.1% for business-critical APIs), Saturation (CPU, memory, connection pool utilisation — alert before exhaustion). Define SLIs (measurable metrics) and SLOs (targets — e.g. 99.9% of requests under 500ms). Configure Prometheus scraping and build Grafana dashboards covering all golden signals. Set up alerts in stages: critical alerts (service down, error rate above 5%) page on-call immediately via PagerDuty. Warning alerts (latency degrading, pool above 70%) notify Slack for awareness. Include deployment events as dashboard annotations to correlate metric changes with releases. Create synthetic monitoring with scheduled health check calls to detect issues before real users.

items.q: A disk on a production WebSphere server is filling up rapidly. What do you do?
items.hint: df -h and du -sh to identify cause. Clear logs/core dumps/tmp. Root cause: logging misconfiguration or JVM dumps.
items.answer: Immediate action: 'df -h' for volume utilisation then 'du -sh /opt/IBM/WebSphere/*' and 'du -sh /var/log/*' to narrow down the cause. Common culprits: unconfigured log rotation — WebSphere SystemOut.log and GC verbose logs growing without bounds. Java core dumps (javacore.*, Snap.*, heapdump.* files) from previous JVM crashes — safe to archive and delete after reviewing for root cause. Application logs with DEBUG level accidentally enabled in production. Temp files accumulating in WebSphere temp/ directory. Free space safely: compress old logs, archive and delete core dumps after extracting diagnostic information, clear temp directories. Identify which application is logging excessively and temporarily set log level to WARNING. Long-term: implement log rotation, set retention policies, and add disk usage alerts at 70% and 85% to catch issues before they become critical.

items.q: How would you migrate a legacy Java application from WebSphere to OpenShift containers?
items.hint: Assess, containerise, adapt (Liberty or Spring Boot), build pipeline, parallel run, cutover.
items.answer: Migration follows a structured process. Assessment phase: audit WebSphere-specific dependencies — EJBs, JMS via WAS connection factories, WAS-proprietary security APIs. Choose container runtime: IBM WebSphere Liberty (supports JEE features in a container — minimal refactoring), IBM Open Liberty (open-source), or refactor to Spring Boot on embedded Tomcat (higher effort, fully cloud-native). Containerisation: write Dockerfile using official Liberty or Tomcat base image, configure JDBC datasources as environment variables or Liberty server.xml with env variable references, externalise all configuration. CI/CD pipeline: Maven build → Docker image → Artifactory push → Helm deploy to OpenShift dev. Test phase: functional parity testing, performance comparison, security scan. Cutover: run parallel for 2 weeks minimum, shift read traffic first then write traffic, maintain WebSphere rollback capability until full confidence is established.

items.q: Development team wants to add a new microservice. What considerations do you raise?
items.hint: CI/CD pipeline, container, namespace/RBAC, resource limits, monitoring, logging, secrets management, network policy.
items.answer: Platform considerations: CI/CD pipeline — create Jenkinsfile (or reuse shared pipeline), configure Bitbucket webhooks, branch protection, and PR policies. Container — Dockerfile follows security baseline (non-root, minimal base image, vulnerability scan passing). OpenShift namespace — provision with appropriate ResourceQuota and LimitRange, create RBAC roles for the development team, configure ImagePullSecret if using external registry. Monitoring — ensure the service exposes /health and /metrics endpoints, create Grafana dashboard covering four golden signals, configure baseline alerts. Logging — confirm logs go to stdout/stderr for OpenShift collection, log format includes correlation IDs for distributed tracing. Secrets — all credentials via Vault or ExternalSecrets, no hardcoded values. Network Policy — define which services can communicate with the new service. Documentation — architecture decision record capturing design choices and dependencies.

items.q: You receive an alert that the Jenkins master is down. What do you do?
items.hint: Check systemd status, Java process, port 8080. Restore from JENKINS_HOME backup or GitOps rebuild. Notify teams.
items.answer: Jenkins outage response: immediately notify development teams that pipelines are impacted. Check Jenkins server: 'systemctl status jenkins'. If stopped, check crash reason in journalctl or /var/log/jenkins/jenkins.log — OutOfMemoryError (increase JVM heap -Xmx in /etc/default/jenkins), port conflict, or disk full (/var/lib/jenkins). Attempt restart: 'systemctl start jenkins'. If it crashes persistently: check disk space, clear old build artifacts, take a heap dump to diagnose OOM. For persistent issues: restore JENKINS_HOME from the most recent backup to a standby VM and update DNS or load balancer. If using JCasC and Job DSL, provision a fresh Jenkins from configuration stored in Git — faster than restoring a large JENKINS_HOME. Throughout recovery keep teams updated via the incident channel with ETA estimates.

items.q: How do you ensure zero-downtime deployment in a clustered environment?
items.hint: Rolling deployment: drain node, deploy, verify health, proceed. Readiness probes, PodDisruptionBudget, feature flags.
items.answer: Zero-downtime deployment strategy: ensure the load balancer has health checks configured to route traffic only to healthy instances. For Kubernetes/OpenShift: configure Rolling Update with maxSurge=1 and maxUnavailable=0 — at least the full desired count of pods serves traffic throughout the update. Set appropriate readinessProbe — Kubernetes only routes traffic to the new pod after readiness passes. PodDisruptionBudget ensures minimum pod count during voluntary disruptions. For VM-based clusters: rolling deployment script iterates through nodes one by one — drain from load balancer, deploy, run automated smoke tests, re-add, proceed to next. Use blue-green for high-risk releases. Feature flags allow deploying code that is disabled at runtime — decoupling deployment from feature release. Database changes must be backward compatible during the transition period when both old and new versions may be running simultaneously.

items.q: A third-party API your application depends on is down. How do you handle it?
items.hint: Circuit breaker pattern, fallback responses, queue for retry, notify stakeholders, check SLA with vendor.
items.answer: Immediate response: confirm the third-party API is down not just your connectivity — check the provider's status page and test from multiple locations. Assess impact: what functionality is broken, can the application degrade gracefully? If circuit breaker is implemented (Resilience4j, Hystrix) it should already be returning fallback responses — verify it is operating correctly. If not: deploy a quick fix catching connection exceptions and returning a cached or default response where possible. Queue operations that can be retried later — put API calls on an MQ queue and process when the third-party recovers. Notify stakeholders of the partial outage with user impact. Contact the vendor's support with their SLA reference number. Document the incident — implement circuit breaker, timeout settings, retry with exponential backoff, and fallback strategies as permanent improvements after the incident is resolved.

items.q: What is Prometheus and what problem does it solve?
items.hint: Open-source monitoring and alerting toolkit. Pull-based metrics collection.
items.answer: Prometheus is an open-source monitoring and alerting system originally built at SoundCloud. It solves the problem of collecting, storing, and querying time-series metrics from distributed systems. Unlike traditional push-based monitoring tools, Prometheus uses a pull model — it scrapes metrics from HTTP endpoints (called targets) at regular intervals. This makes it easy to add monitoring without changing application architecture. It stores metrics in a local time-series database (TSDB) with a powerful query language called PromQL, and integrates with Alertmanager for routing alerts. In a DevOps context, Prometheus is the de facto standard for Kubernetes and OpenShift cluster monitoring.

items.q: Explain the Prometheus architecture and its core components.
items.hint: Prometheus Server, Exporters, Pushgateway, Alertmanager, Service Discovery.
items.answer: Prometheus has five core components. The Prometheus Server is the heart — it scrapes metrics from targets, stores them in the TSDB, and evaluates alerting rules. Exporters are lightweight agents that expose metrics from systems that do not natively speak Prometheus format — for example, node_exporter for Linux OS metrics, blackbox_exporter for endpoint probing, and jmx_exporter for Java/JVM metrics (useful for WebSphere and Tomcat). The Pushgateway is for short-lived jobs that cannot be scraped — they push metrics to the gateway and Prometheus scrapes it. Alertmanager receives firing alerts from Prometheus and handles routing, deduplication, grouping, and notification to Slack, email, PagerDuty, etc. Service Discovery lets Prometheus automatically discover targets in dynamic environments like Kubernetes, AWS EC2, and OpenShift — no manual target configuration needed.

items.q: What is a metric in Prometheus? Explain the four metric types.
items.hint: Counter (ever-increasing), Gauge (up/down), Histogram (buckets), Summary (quantiles).
items.answer: A Prometheus metric is a named, labelled time-series value with a timestamp. The four types are: Counter — a monotonically increasing value that only goes up, used for total counts like HTTP requests, errors, or bytes processed. You never reset a counter; you use rate() in PromQL to get the per-second rate. Gauge — a value that can go up or down, used for current state like CPU usage, memory usage, number of active connections, or queue depth. Histogram — samples observations (like request durations) and places them into configurable buckets, plus a sum and count. It lets you calculate percentiles server-side with histogram_quantile(). Summary — similar to Histogram but calculates quantiles client-side (in the application). Histograms are preferred in Prometheus because they can be aggregated across multiple instances, while Summaries cannot.

items.q: What is PromQL? Give examples of common queries you have used.
items.hint: Prometheus Query Language. rate(), irate(), increase(), histogram_quantile(), by(), without().
items.answer: PromQL is Prometheus's functional query language used to select and aggregate time-series data. Common patterns I use: 'rate(http_requests_total[5m])' gives the per-second HTTP request rate averaged over 5 minutes — always use rate() on counters, not raw values. 'irate(http_requests_total[5m])' gives the instantaneous rate, better for volatile spikes. 'histogram_quantile(0.95, rate(http_request_duration_seconds_bucket[5m]))' calculates the 95th percentile latency — critical for SLA monitoring. 'node_memory_MemAvailable_bytes / node_memory_MemTotal_bytes * 100' shows available memory percentage on a node. 'sum(rate(http_requests_total[5m])) by (service)' aggregates request rate grouped by service. 'absent(up{job="api"})' fires an alert when a target completely disappears from Prometheus — extremely useful for detecting crashed services.

items.q: What are labels in Prometheus and why are they important?
items.hint: Key-value pairs that identify dimensions of a metric. Cardinality risk if misused.
items.answer: Labels are key-value pairs attached to metrics that add dimensional context. For example, 'http_requests_total{method="GET", status="200", service="payment-api"}' — you can now query and aggregate by any combination of these labels. Labels are what make a single metric multidimensional. However, cardinality is the biggest risk with labels — each unique combination of label values creates a new time series. If you add a label with unbounded values like user_id or request_id, you can create millions of time series and crash Prometheus. The rule of thumb is labels should have low cardinality (a small, bounded set of values). Good label examples: environment (prod/staging), region (us-east/eu-west), pod name, service name. Bad label examples: user ID, order ID, IP address.

items.q: How does Prometheus service discovery work in Kubernetes/OpenShift?
items.hint: kubernetes_sd_configs. Automatically discovers Pods, Services, Nodes via Kubernetes API.
items.answer: In Kubernetes and OpenShift, Prometheus uses kubernetes_sd_configs to automatically discover scrape targets via the Kubernetes API. It can discover Pods, Services, Endpoints, Nodes, and Ingresses. When a new Pod starts, Prometheus discovers it automatically — no manual prometheus.yml changes needed. You control which Pods are scraped using annotations: 'prometheus.io/scrape: "true"' opts a Pod in, 'prometheus.io/port: "8080"' specifies the metrics port, and 'prometheus.io/path: "/metrics"' overrides the default path. Relabeling rules (relabel_configs) in the scrape config let you filter targets, rename labels, or drop metrics before they are stored. In OpenShift, the Prometheus Operator uses ServiceMonitor and PodMonitor CRDs to define scrape configurations declaratively — no raw prometheus.yml editing required. This is the production-standard approach.

items.q: What is a ServiceMonitor in Prometheus Operator?
items.hint: Kubernetes CRD that declaratively defines which Services Prometheus should scrape.
items.answer: The Prometheus Operator extends Kubernetes with Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) to manage Prometheus configuration declaratively. A ServiceMonitor is a CRD that specifies which Kubernetes Services Prometheus should scrape and how. Instead of editing prometheus.yml directly, you create a ServiceMonitor YAML that references a Service using label selectors, defines the port name, scrape interval, and path. The Prometheus Operator watches for ServiceMonitor objects and automatically updates the Prometheus configuration. This fits perfectly into GitOps workflows — your monitoring config lives alongside your application manifests in Git. For example, when I deploy a new microservice on OpenShift, I include a ServiceMonitor in the Helm chart so Prometheus automatically starts scraping metrics the moment the service is deployed.

items.q: How do you configure alerting in Prometheus?
items.hint: PrometheusRule CRD or alert.rules file. Alertmanager routes and sends notifications.
items.answer: Alerting in Prometheus has two parts. First, you define alerting rules in Prometheus (as YAML files or PrometheusRule CRDs in Operator setups). A rule has a PromQL expression, a 'for' duration (how long the condition must be true before firing), severity labels, and annotation text. For example: 'alert: HighMemoryUsage, expr: node_memory_MemAvailable_bytes / node_memory_MemTotal_bytes < 0.10, for: 5m, labels: {severity: critical}, annotations: {summary: "Memory below 10% on {{ $labels.instance }}"}'. When the expression is true for 5 consecutive minutes, Prometheus sends the alert to Alertmanager. Second, Alertmanager handles routing — it deduplicates alerts, groups related alerts, applies silences, and routes to receivers like Slack, email, or PagerDuty based on label matchers in the routing tree. In OpenShift, PrometheusRule CRDs make alert management fully GitOps-compatible.

items.q: What is the Pushgateway and when should you use it?
items.hint: For short-lived jobs (batch, cron) that exit before Prometheus can scrape them.
items.answer: The Pushgateway is an intermediary service for jobs that cannot be scraped because they are short-lived — they finish and exit before Prometheus's next scrape cycle. Batch jobs, cron jobs, and one-off scripts push their metrics to the Pushgateway on completion, and Prometheus then scrapes the gateway on its normal schedule. The correct use case is specifically short-lived jobs — for example, a database backup job that runs at 2am, completes in 3 minutes, then pushes metrics like backup_duration_seconds and backup_success (1 or 0) to the gateway. Importantly, the Pushgateway is NOT a general-purpose push endpoint for all services. Long-running services should expose a /metrics endpoint and be scraped normally. A common anti-pattern is using Pushgateway for everything, which defeats Prometheus's pull model and breaks the instance-up detection (if a service dies, Prometheus still sees stale metrics from the gateway).

items.q: How do you handle Prometheus storage and retention?
items.hint: Local TSDB with --storage.tsdb.retention.time. Remote storage for long-term (Thanos, Cortex).
items.answer: Prometheus stores data locally in its TSDB. Retention is controlled by the --storage.tsdb.retention.time flag (default 15 days) or --storage.tsdb.retention.size to cap by disk size. For local retention this is fine, but for long-term storage (months/years) and high availability, Prometheus integrates with remote storage solutions. Thanos is the most popular — it runs as sidecar containers alongside Prometheus, uploads TSDB blocks to object storage (S3, GCS, Azure Blob), and provides a global query layer across multiple Prometheus instances. Cortex is another option offering multi-tenant long-term storage. In OpenShift environments, the platform's built-in Prometheus stack often uses Thanos components by default for HA. For capacity planning, I monitor TSDB size with 'prometheus_tsdb_head_chunks' and 'prometheus_tsdb_storage_blocks_bytes' metrics — Prometheus monitoring itself.

items.q: What is a recording rule in Prometheus and why would you use it?
items.hint: Pre-computes expensive PromQL expressions and saves result as a new metric.
items.answer: Recording rules allow you to pre-compute frequently used or computationally expensive PromQL queries and save the result as a new time-series metric. This has two main benefits: performance and simplicity. For dashboards that run the same heavy query every time they refresh (e.g., a 95th percentile calculation across hundreds of instances), pre-computing it as a recording rule means Grafana just queries a simple metric instead of re-running the expensive expression on every panel refresh. The second benefit is simplifying complex expressions — you can create a hierarchy of recording rules where simpler rules feed into more complex ones. Recording rules are defined in the same rules YAML files as alerting rules, using 'record: job:metric:aggregation' naming convention. For example: 'record: job:http_request_duration_seconds:p95, expr: histogram_quantile(0.95, sum(rate(http_request_duration_seconds_bucket[5m])) by (le, job))'. This pre-computed metric is then trivial to query and plot.

items.q: How do you monitor a Java application (like WebSphere or Tomcat) with Prometheus?
items.hint: JMX Exporter as Java agent exposes JVM and app metrics on /metrics endpoint.
items.answer: Java applications expose management data via JMX (Java Management Extensions). The Prometheus JMX Exporter bridges JMX to Prometheus format — it runs either as a Java agent (attached to the JVM at startup) or as a standalone process. As a Java agent, you add it to the JVM startup arguments: '-javaagent:/path/to/jmx_prometheus_javaagent.jar=9404:/path/to/config.yaml'. The config YAML maps JMX MBeans to Prometheus metric names. For Tomcat, important metrics include heap memory (jvm_memory_bytes_used), GC pause time (jvm_gc_collection_seconds), thread pool (tomcat_threads_current), and active sessions. For WebSphere, you monitor thread pools, JDBC connection pools, and transaction rates via JMX MBeans. Once the exporter is running, Prometheus scrapes the /metrics endpoint on port 9404 just like any other target. This is the approach I used at FIS to bring JVM visibility into the Prometheus/Grafana stack for core banking middleware.

items.q: What is Grafana and how does it complement Prometheus?
items.hint: Grafana is a visualization platform. Prometheus stores metrics; Grafana queries and visualises them.
items.answer: Grafana is an open-source analytics and visualization platform. While Prometheus excels at collecting, storing, and alerting on metrics, its built-in graph UI is basic and not suitable for production dashboards. Grafana fills this gap — it connects to Prometheus (and many other data sources like Loki, Elasticsearch, InfluxDB, CloudWatch, Azure Monitor) as a backend and provides rich, interactive dashboards. You write PromQL queries in Grafana panels, and Grafana renders them as graphs, gauges, heatmaps, tables, and stat panels. Grafana also has its own alerting system, user management, and dashboard sharing. In a PLG stack (Prometheus + Loki + Grafana), Grafana becomes the single pane of glass — metrics from Prometheus, logs from Loki, and traces from Tempo all visible in one place. This unified observability view is what modern DevOps teams need.

items.q: What are the main components of a Grafana dashboard?
items.hint: Dashboard > Rows > Panels. Each panel has a data source, query, and visualization type.
items.answer: A Grafana dashboard is organised hierarchically. At the top level is the Dashboard itself, which has a title, tags, time range picker, and refresh interval. Dashboards contain Rows, which are collapsible horizontal sections used to group related panels (e.g., a 'CPU & Memory' row and a 'Network' row). Each Row contains Panels, which are the individual visualisation units. A panel has three parts: the Data Source (which Prometheus instance or other backend to query), the Query (PromQL expression), and the Visualisation type. Panel types include Time series (line/bar charts for trends), Stat (single big number for current value), Gauge (progress bar-style for percentage metrics), Bar chart (category comparisons), Heatmap (distribution over time), Table (tabular data), Text (markdown annotations), and Logs (for Loki log streams). Dashboard Variables are a key feature — they let you create dropdowns (like environment, namespace, pod) that dynamically filter all panels at once.

items.q: What are Grafana Variables and how do you use them?
items.hint: Template variables create dropdowns. Query-type variables populate from metric label values.
items.answer: Grafana Variables (also called template variables) make dashboards dynamic and reusable across environments, namespaces, and services. Instead of hardcoding a server name in every panel query, you define a variable like '$instance' and reference it in all queries — the user selects from a dropdown and all panels update simultaneously. The most powerful type is the Query variable, which populates its values by querying your data source. For example, a variable using 'label_values(node_uname_info, instance)' automatically lists all Prometheus nodes as dropdown options. Variable types: Query (from data source), Custom (manually defined list), Constant (fixed value, often for data source URLs), Datasource (switch between Prometheus instances), Interval (for time grouping), and Text box (free input). I use chained variables heavily — a 'namespace' variable filters a 'pod' variable so the pod dropdown only shows pods in the selected namespace. This is done with regex filtering referencing the upstream variable.

items.q: How do you import and manage Grafana dashboards?
items.hint: Import by JSON, Grafana.com ID, or provision via YAML config files.
items.answer: Grafana dashboards can be managed three ways. Manual import — in the UI, go to Dashboards > Import and paste a JSON or enter a Grafana.com dashboard ID. Grafana.com hosts thousands of community dashboards (e.g., ID 1860 is the widely-used Node Exporter Full dashboard, ID 315 is for Kubernetes cluster monitoring). This is the quickest way to get a production-quality dashboard immediately. JSON export/import — every Grafana dashboard can be exported as JSON from Settings > JSON Model. You can version-control this JSON in Git and import it to other Grafana instances. Dashboard provisioning — the production-grade approach. You place dashboard JSON files in a provisioning directory and create a YAML config file at 'provisioning/dashboards/'. Grafana loads them automatically on startup. This makes dashboards part of your infrastructure-as-code — they are deployed via Helm or ConfigMaps in OpenShift, not clicked through the UI. Changes go through Git review like any other config.

items.q: Explain Grafana data source configuration. What sources have you used?
items.hint: Data sources are backends Grafana queries. Prometheus, Loki, CloudWatch, Azure Monitor, Elasticsearch.
items.answer: A Grafana data source is a configured connection to a backend system that Grafana queries for data. You configure data sources in Configuration > Data Sources, providing the URL, authentication, and source-specific settings. Data sources I have worked with: Prometheus — for metrics, the primary source in Kubernetes/OpenShift environments; you provide the Prometheus server URL and optionally set scrape interval hints. Loki — for log aggregation (part of the PLG stack); uses LogQL query language similar to PromQL. CloudWatch — for AWS metrics like EC2, RDS, Lambda; requires AWS credentials. Azure Monitor — for Azure resource metrics; uses service principal authentication. Elasticsearch — for log and event search. In production, data sources should be provisioned via YAML files rather than configured through the UI, so they are consistent across all environments. Multiple data sources can be used in the same dashboard — for example, correlating Prometheus metrics with Loki logs on the same time axis.

items.q: What is Grafana alerting and how does it compare to Prometheus Alertmanager?
items.hint: Grafana has its own alerting engine. Can alert on any data source, not just Prometheus.
items.answer: Grafana has a built-in alerting system (significantly improved in Grafana 8+, now called Grafana Alerting or Unified Alerting). Grafana alert rules are defined on panels and can query any data source — this is its key advantage over Prometheus alerting, which is limited to Prometheus metrics. With Grafana Alerting you can alert on Loki log queries, CloudWatch metrics, or Elasticsearch data, not just Prometheus. Grafana Alerting uses a similar concept to Alertmanager: contact points (Slack, email, PagerDuty) and notification policies for routing. However, in most production Kubernetes setups, Prometheus Alertmanager remains preferred for infrastructure alerts (node down, pod crash, high CPU) because it is closer to the data, supports complex inhibition and silencing rules, and is GitOps-manageable via PrometheusRule CRDs. Grafana Alerting is preferred for business-level alerts that span multiple data sources. The two systems can coexist — Grafana can even use Alertmanager as its notification backend.

items.q: How do you organise Grafana dashboards in a large team environment?
items.hint: Folders for organisation, Teams for access control, dashboard provisioning for GitOps.
items.answer: In a large team environment, uncontrolled dashboard sprawl is a common problem. Organisation strategies: Folders — group dashboards by team, service, or platform (e.g., 'Platform/Infrastructure', 'App Team/Payment Service', 'SRE/SLO Dashboards'). Teams and permissions — Grafana Teams let you assign folder-level permissions so each team can only edit their own dashboards while viewing others. Dashboard ownership — every dashboard should have a team owner defined in its metadata. Naming conventions — prefix dashboard names with team or service ('[Infra] Node Overview', '[Payments] API Latency'). Dashboard provisioning via Git — the most important practice at scale. All production dashboards are stored as JSON in a Git repo, reviewed via PR, and deployed to Grafana automatically via provisioning. This prevents 'rogue dashboards' created manually in prod, ensures dashboards are reproducible across environments, and gives you history of every change. Dashboards provisioned this way are read-only in the UI — changes must go through Git.

items.q: What is Loki and how does it integrate with Grafana in a PLG stack?
items.hint: Loki is like Prometheus for logs. Indexes labels not log content. Uses LogQL. Grafana visualises both.
items.answer: Loki is a horizontally scalable, highly available log aggregation system built by Grafana Labs, designed to be cost-effective by indexing only labels (metadata) rather than full log content. This is its key differentiator from Elasticsearch — Loki does not index the log text, only the labels attached to log streams (like pod name, namespace, container). Log content is stored compressed and queried with grep-like full-text search. Promtail is the log collection agent — similar to node_exporter for metrics, it runs on every node, tails log files, attaches Kubernetes labels, and ships to Loki. Grafana integrates with Loki as a data source using LogQL query language. A typical LogQL query: '{namespace="banking", container="payment-api"} |= "ERROR" | json | latency > 500'. In a PLG stack (Prometheus + Loki + Grafana), the power is correlation — you can click on a spike in a Prometheus metric graph and jump directly to the Loki logs from that time window and pod. This dramatically reduces mean time to diagnose issues.

items.q: How would you set up monitoring for an OpenShift cluster using Prometheus and Grafana?
items.hint: OpenShift has built-in monitoring stack. Prometheus Operator, ServiceMonitors, cluster-monitoring-config.
items.answer: OpenShift ships with a built-in monitoring stack based on the Prometheus Operator (found in the openshift-monitoring namespace). It includes Prometheus, Alertmanager, Thanos components for HA, and Grafana (though newer versions use the OpenShift Console built-in metrics UI). To extend monitoring for your own applications: first enable user workload monitoring by editing the cluster-monitoring-config ConfigMap in openshift-monitoring — set 'enableUserWorkload: true'. This creates a separate Prometheus instance in openshift-user-workload-monitoring namespace for application metrics. Then deploy ServiceMonitor CRDs in your application namespace — Prometheus automatically discovers and scrapes them. For custom Grafana, deploy it via the Grafana Operator from OperatorHub and configure GrafanaDataSource CRDs pointing to the Thanos Querier endpoint (not directly to Prometheus, as Thanos Querier provides the federated view). Add the cluster CA certificate for TLS. Then provision dashboards as GrafanaDashboard CRDs — all fully GitOps compatible. This gives you cluster metrics, node metrics, and application metrics all in one Grafana instance.

items.q: What are some Grafana best practices for production dashboards?
items.hint: USE/RED methods, meaningful thresholds, version-control JSON, avoid too many panels.
items.answer: Production dashboard best practices I follow: Use the USE and RED methods as frameworks. USE (Utilization, Saturation, Errors) for infrastructure resources — CPU, memory, disk, network. RED (Rate, Errors, Duration) for services — requests per second, error rate, latency percentiles. Start every dashboard with a summary row — 3-4 stat panels showing the most critical numbers (error rate, p95 latency, pod count, alert count) so on-call engineers get the health status at a glance. Set meaningful thresholds — colour stat and gauge panels with green/yellow/red thresholds tied to your actual SLOs, not arbitrary values. Always use rate() on counters — never plot raw counter values, they only go up and tell you nothing useful as an instant value. Use dashboard links to connect related dashboards — link from a service dashboard to the underlying node dashboard. Keep panel count reasonable — a dashboard with 50 panels loads slowly and overwhelms the viewer. Use rows with collapse to hide detail panels. Always set default time range and refresh interval appropriate to the dashboard purpose (1 minute refresh for on-call dashboards, 5 minutes for trend dashboards). Store all dashboards in Git.
